OFFICIAL EARNINGS DETAIL

Revenue / Sales:
Q1 revenue was $35.90B in USD terms, up 40.6% YoY and 6.4% QoQ. In NT dollars, revenue was NT$1.134T, up 35.1% YoY and 8.4% QoQ.

Consensus:
ADR EPS consensus was around $3.35, while reported ADR EPS was approximately $3.49. Revenue exceeded the high end of management’s prior Q1 guide.

EPS / Earnings:
Net income was NT$572.5B, up 58.3% YoY. Diluted EPS was NT$22.08.

Profitability / Margins:
Gross margin was 66.2% versus guidance of 63%-65%. Operating margin was 58.1% versus guidance of 54%-56%. Margin expansion was the cleanest incremental signal in the print.

Key KPI:
Advanced nodes accounted for 74% of wafer revenue, including 3nm at 25%, 5nm at 36% and 7nm at 13%. HPC accounted for 61% of revenue, making AI / data-center the dominant demand engine.

Guidance headline:
Q2 revenue was guided to $39.0B-$40.2B, gross margin to 65.5%-67.5% and operating margin to 56.5%-58.5%. Full-year USD revenue growth was upgraded to more than 30%, and capex is expected toward the high end of the $52B-$56B range.

Capital allocation:
Capex remains the capital allocation story. Q1 capex was $11.1B and management continues to signal an elevated multi-year investment cycle, including Arizona expansion.

Other relevant official details:
Management flagged Middle East-linked chemical and gas input-cost risk, but not an immediate operational disruption. The risk channel is cost and input availability, not end-demand.

SEGMENT / BUSINESS BREAKDOWN

SEGMENT TABLE

| Segment | % of Total | Revenue | YoY Growth | Margin Trend | Key Commentary |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| High Performance Computing | 61% | ~$21.9B | Strong | Very strong | AI accelerators and data-center demand are the main growth engine. |
| Smartphone | 26% | ~$9.3B | Secondary | Stable | No longer the dominant driver of the equity story. |
| IoT | 6% | ~$2.2B | Modest | Stable | Small contribution. |

COMMENTARY

The mix is decisive: TSMC is now effectively an AI / HPC capacity platform with a smartphone business attached, rather than a smartphone-cycle proxy with some AI upside. Advanced-node mix and utilization explain the margin beat.

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

The FY revenue growth upgrade to more than 30% is modest in wording but meaningful in signal. Management is not just defending the prior AI thesis; it is indicating improved visibility.

Capex at the high end of $52B-$56B and the Arizona investment program confirm that supply remains the constraint. The risk is execution and input cost, not order weakness.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

The professional tape framed the quarter as confirmation of an AI sold-out environment. Commentary emphasized that demand continues to exceed available leading-edge capacity and that margin upside is being supported by utilization and pricing power.

External commentary also identified resource constraints as the defining feature of the 2026 semiconductor cycle. For TSMC, this means the equity debate is not whether AI demand exists, but how quickly capacity, chemicals, gases, power, equipment and overseas fabs can scale.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality part of the print was the margin beat. Revenue strength was partly pre-announced through monthly sales, but 66.2% gross margin and 58.1% operating margin show that TSMC has pricing power and utilization leverage.

The lowest-quality risk is not the current quarter; it is concentration and execution. HPC at 61% of revenue makes the company more exposed to AI capex digestion risk if hyperscaler spending ever slows. For now, the evidence points the other way.

State transition: Continuation

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether AI demand was beginning to plateau or whether supply remained the binding constraint. Q1 strongly confirms the supply-constraint interpretation. The thesis is unchanged but reinforced: TSMC is still the scarce capacity layer in the AI infrastructure stack.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

This is not a classic cyclical semi beat. The market is pricing TSMC as the bottleneck asset for AI infrastructure, and Q1 validates that framing through HPC mix, margin upside and upgraded FY revenue growth.

The reason the stock reaction was not explosive is setup, not weakness. The April revenue release had already de-risked the topline, and geopolitical discount remains embedded. The incremental positives were margin quality and the FY upgrade.

The main risk to monitor is whether capex, input-cost inflation or geopolitics start to impair margin conversion. Until then, TSMC remains the cleanest read-through for AI capex durability.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

The ADR reaction was modestly positive rather than euphoric. That is consistent with a strong but partially pre-signalled print.

Peer read-across is strongly positive for advanced semiconductor equipment, AI accelerators, HBM / packaging supply chains and data-center infrastructure. It is less directly positive for consumer semi, because HPC is the dominant revenue driver.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

AI capacity tightness
Why it matters: The bull case depends on demand staying above supply, not merely on headline revenue growth.

Gross margin durability
Why it matters: Margins are the proof that TSMC has pricing power and utilization leverage.

Capex / Arizona execution
Why it matters: Supply expansion is the constraint; delays, cost inflation or yield issues would matter more than small demand fluctuations.

BOTTOM LINE

Very strong AI foundry quarter.
The business remains in structural continuation rather than new inflection because the AI thesis was already established.
The next issue is whether TSMC can sustain 65%+ gross margins while scaling capacity aggressively.

Profitability / Margins:

The key KPI was North America food volume. PepsiCo Foods North America delivered volume growth after several weak quarters, which supports the idea that affordability and innovation actions are beginning to work.

COMMENTARY

This is a stabilization print, not a full reacceleration. The key positive is that the problem segment, North America foods, showed volume recovery after pricing and affordability actions. The caveat is that organic revenue growth of 2.6% is not a high-growth signal and reported growth was materially helped by FX and portfolio effects.

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

The guidance hold matters because the market was worried that weaker consumer demand and value-seeking behavior would force a reset. Management reaffirmed 2%-4% organic revenue growth and 4%-6% core constant-currency EPS growth.

Capital return remains a defensive anchor. The 54-year dividend growth streak and almost $9B planned shareholder return support the quality / yield buyer base even if top-line growth remains modest.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

The professional tape focused on PepsiCo’s price and affordability actions. Coverage noted that cutting prices on some snack lines helped revive demand, especially among cost-conscious consumers.

The key market interpretation was that Q1 reduced the bear case around North America snacks. The company is not suddenly a high-growth consumer staple, but the volume recovery suggests the elasticity problem may be manageable.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality part of the print was PFNA volume recovery. That is the line investors needed to see because snacks had been under pressure from high pricing, consumer fatigue and trade-down.

The lowest-quality part was the gap between reported and organic growth. 8.5% reported revenue growth looks strong, but 2.6% organic growth is the cleaner underlying read.

State transition: Stabilization

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether PepsiCo had over-earned on pricing and lost volume momentum in North America snacks. Q1 shows the first concrete evidence that affordability actions can stabilize volume. The thesis is not fully reset, but the downside narrative has weakened.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

For PM read-across, PEP is a useful signal on the U.S. value-sensitive consumer. The consumer is not rejecting branded snacks entirely; the consumer is responding to affordability and innovation.

The stock can hold a defensive multiple if volume keeps recovering and guidance remains intact. A bigger rerating would require organic growth to move toward the upper end of guidance without sacrificing margins.

The main risk is that Q1 reflects promotional pull-forward rather than durable volume repair. The next test is whether PFNA can sustain volume recovery after the initial price-reset benefit.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

Shares rose after the release as investors rewarded the revenue beat, guidance reaffirmation and North America food volume recovery.

Peer read-across: positive for branded staples that can regain volume through affordability, packaging and innovation; negative for companies still relying only on price with weak elasticities.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

PFNA volume durability
Why it matters: This is the core bear-case reversal point.

Organic revenue acceleration
Why it matters: Reported revenue was helped by FX and M&A; organic growth must carry the thesis.

Margin discipline after price cuts
Why it matters: Affordability works only if it restores volume without destroying profit conversion.

BOTTOM LINE

Good stabilization quarter.
The market rewarded the return of food volume and the guidance hold.
The next issue is whether the affordability reset produces durable organic growth, not just one quarter of relief.

<<PEP_EARNINGS_END>>

OFFICIAL EARNINGS DETAIL

Revenue / Sales:
Net revenues were $6.482B, up 16% YoY and a quarterly record. Net interest revenue, asset management and administration fees, and trading revenue all grew YoY.

Consensus:
Revenue consensus was around $6.50B, so revenue was slightly below expectation. Adjusted EPS consensus was around $1.40, so adjusted EPS beat.

EPS / Earnings:
GAAP net income was $2.479B and GAAP EPS was $1.37. Adjusted net income was $2.588B and adjusted EPS was $1.43, up 38% YoY.

Profitability / Margins:
GAAP pre-tax profit margin was 47.1% and adjusted pre-tax profit margin was 49.4%. Profitability improved strongly from Q1 2025.

Key KPI:
Total client assets were $11.77T, up 19% YoY. Core net new assets were $140B, or $157.5B excluding a planned mutual fund clearing deconversion. Daily average trades reached a record 9.9M, up 34% YoY.

Guidance headline:
Management commentary indicated EPS was tracking above the previously implied range, assuming current rates and sustained client engagement.

Capital allocation:
Schwab repurchased $2.4B of common stock and raised the quarterly dividend by 19% to $0.32 per share.

Other relevant official details:
Schwab announced plans to expand into spot crypto exposure for clients and framed this as part of a broader platform-extension strategy, including future tokenization and AI-enabled client tools.

SEGMENT / BUSINESS BREAKDOWN

SEGMENT TABLE

| Segment | % of Total | Revenue | YoY Growth | Margin Trend | Key Commentary |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| Net Interest Revenue | 48% | $3.143B | +16% | Mixed | Improved YoY but missed expectations and slightly declined QoQ. |
| Asset Management / Administration Fees | 27% | $1.759B | +15% | Strong | Asset growth and managed investing flows helped. |
| Trading Revenue | 17% | $1.089B | +20% | Strong | Record 9.9M daily average trades. |
| Bank Deposit Account Fees | 5% | $295M | +20% | Stable | Smaller but supportive fee source. |
| Other Revenue | 3% | $195M | -7% | Not meaningful | Not thesis-driving. |

COMMENTARY

The operating print was strong, but the stock reaction reveals the real valuation issue. Investors are not disputing client asset growth, trading activity or EPS delivery. They are questioning the durability of net interest revenue and the vulnerability of sweep cash to competition, AI-enabled cash tools and higher-yield alternatives.

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

Capital allocation was shareholder-friendly, with $2.4B of buybacks and a 19% dividend increase. That reinforced management confidence in normalized earnings power.

The forward guide is less about formal revenue targets and more about NIR normalization. If sweep cash stabilizes and client engagement remains high, Schwab’s earnings recovery can continue. If cash sorting or cash-sweep disruption resumes, the rerating stalls.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

Professional commentary framed Q1 as a record operating quarter that nevertheless failed to satisfy investors because revenue and NIR were slightly below expectations. Barron’s and other market coverage highlighted the stock selloff despite record EPS.

The more important narrative was cash sweep vulnerability. Investors became concerned that AI-enabled cash management tools could disrupt Schwab’s ability to monetize low-yield sweep balances, even though this remains more of a medium-term competitive risk than a Q1 operating fact.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality part of the print was client engagement. $11.77T of assets, 1.3M new brokerage accounts, record daily average trades and strong core net new assets show that the platform remains highly relevant.

The lowest-quality part was NIR optics. Net interest revenue grew strongly YoY, but it was slightly below expectations and down sequentially, keeping cash sorting and sweep monetization at the center of the debate.

State transition: Improvement

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether post-TD integration and cash sorting would continue to suppress Schwab’s earnings power. Q1 shows strong normalization in client activity and EPS, but not a full clearance of cash-sweep risk. The thesis is improving, but the market still needs NIR stabilization.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

This is a good example of why the stock can fall on a record quarter. Schwab delivered EPS and engagement, but investors are pricing the forward cash-sweep model, not just Q1 activity.

The upside case is intact if sweep cash stabilizes, trading remains elevated and asset management fees compound with client assets. The downside risk is that competitive cash management, AI tools or renewed sorting compress the NIR engine faster than expected.

PM read: operational improvement is real, but the multiple will not fully rerate until cash monetization looks safer.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

The stock sold off after earnings despite record EPS and strong client metrics. The market focused on the slight revenue / NIR miss and potential disruption to sweep cash economics.

Peer read-across: positive for brokerage activity and retail engagement; negative for platforms with material sweep-cash monetization exposure if competitive cash tools gain traction.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

Sweep cash stabilization
Why it matters: This is the central swing factor for NIR and valuation.

Client trading activity
Why it matters: Record DATs supported revenue; a volatility slowdown would reduce the cyclical tailwind.

AI / cash-management competition
Why it matters: The market is starting to price potential disruption to low-yield sweep balances.

BOTTOM LINE

Strong operating quarter, weak stock reaction.
Schwab is moving toward normalized earnings power, but cash-sweep risk still dominates the multiple.
The next issue is whether NIR stabilizes enough to confirm that cash sorting is really behind the company.

The quarter did not create a new guidance problem. Management’s focus remains on durable positive operating leverage, fee diversification and strategic capital markets expansion through BTIG.

Capital is healthy, but the stock reaction suggests investors want more visible acceleration before rewarding the name.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

Professional commentary framed USB as a beat with limited stock excitement. Reuters highlighted profit growth from higher NII and fee revenue, while market commentary noted the stock dipped despite the beat.

The broader sector issue is that steady regional-bank execution is not enough when investors remain cautious about NII durability, credit and deposit competition.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality part of the print was fee diversification. Capital markets revenue up around 30% and fee revenue up 6.9% reduce the reliance on NII.

The lowest-quality issue is catalyst absence. NIM was flat, not expanding, and the quarter did not include a major guide raise or capital return surprise.

State transition: Stabilization

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether super-regionals could maintain earnings resilience without a major rate tailwind. USB confirms stable execution, fee diversification and cost discipline. It does not yet prove a breakout earnings acceleration.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

USB is a credible low-drama regional-bank execution story. That has value in a market still wary of credit and deposit risk.

But for the stock to rerate, steady execution must become visible acceleration. That likely means NII growth, stronger fee growth, or proof that BTIG meaningfully lifts the capital markets platform.

PM read: downside is supported by stability, but upside needs a clearer catalyst.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

The stock traded slightly lower despite the EPS beat, consistent with a market that viewed the print as steady but not thesis-changing.

Peer read-across: positive for fee-diversified super-regionals; neutral for pure spread banks because USB did not deliver a major NIM expansion signal.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

Positive operating leverage
Why it matters: It is USB’s clearest execution proof point.

Fee diversification
Why it matters: Stronger capital markets and payments revenue reduce NII dependency.

NII acceleration
Why it matters: Without stronger NII, the stock may remain a stable but low-catalyst bank.

BOTTOM LINE

Steady quarter.
USB executed well, but the print was not strong enough to force a rerating.
The next issue is whether fee growth and NII can accelerate beyond “credible but boring.”

OFFICIAL EARNINGS DETAIL

Total revenue was $3.8B, up 16% YoY and a record. Fee revenue was approximately $3.0B, up 15% YoY, and NII was $835M, up 17% YoY.

Consensus:
Revenue exceeded consensus estimates around $3.63B-$3.66B. EPS excluding notable items of $2.84 beat consensus around $2.60-$2.64.

EPS / Earnings:
Reported EPS was $2.49, up 22% YoY. EPS excluding notable items was $2.84, up 39% YoY.

Profitability / Margins:
ROTCE was approximately 20%, up around 4pp YoY. Pretax margin expanded, and the company delivered another quarter of positive operating leverage.

Key KPI:
AUC/A reached $54.5T and AUM reached $5.6T. Servicing fees, management fees, FX trading and NII all grew YoY.

Guidance headline:
Management raised 2026 fee revenue growth guidance to 7%-9% from 4%-6% and NII growth guidance to 8%-10% from low single digits. Expense growth guidance also moved up to 5%-6% from 3%-4%.

Capital allocation:
State Street returned $633M to shareholders, including $400M of buybacks and $233M of dividends.

Other relevant official details:
Management highlighted volatility, FX activity and institutional client flows as key contributors. AI and digital asset tokenization remain strategic operating initiatives.

SEGMENT / BUSINESS BREAKDOWN

SEGMENT TABLE

| Segment | % of Total | Revenue | YoY Growth | Margin Trend | Key Commentary |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| Servicing Fees | 37% | ~$1.4B | +11% | Stable | Core asset servicing platform benefited from AUC/A growth and activity. |
| Management Fees | 19% | $724M | +23% | Strong | Market levels and flows supported growth. |
| FX Trading Services | 11% | $435M | +29% | Strong | Volatility and client rebalancing drove upside. |
| Net Interest Income | 22% | $835M | +17% | Improving | Balance-sheet and rate tailwinds supported NII. |

COMMENTARY

The quarter was broad and stronger than a single-line FX benefit. Servicing, management fees, FX and NII all grew, while AUC/A and AUM reached record or very strong levels. The offset is expense guidance: growth is strong enough to lift revenue guidance, but also strong enough to raise revenue-related costs.

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

The dual guide upgrade is the main event. Fee revenue and NII guidance both moved higher, which is unusual and points to broad momentum rather than a narrow beat.

Expense guidance also moved higher to 5%-6%, which limits the operating leverage purity. Still, if revenue guidance is delivered, State Street should maintain a positive earnings trajectory.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

Reuters framed the result as a custody-bank profit increase driven by robust fee income, market volatility, geopolitical tensions and higher client activity. Market coverage also emphasized State Street’s read-across to BNY Mellon, which had similarly reported strength in custody / servicing activity.

The professional tape is therefore consistent: custodian banks are beneficiaries of elevated institutional market activity, not victims of volatility.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality part of the print was the guidance upgrade. A one-quarter FX trading spike would be less important; raising fee and NII growth guidance makes the quarter more structural.

The lowest-quality caveat is expense growth. Revenue-linked and investment spending mean that not all of the top-line upside will fall to the bottom line.

State transition: Improvement

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether custody banks could benefit from volatility and activity without cost inflation absorbing the upside. Q1 says yes, with broad fee and NII strength. The thesis improves, but expense discipline remains the key monitor.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

State Street is a clean read on institutional market activity. Volatility, FX trading, portfolio rebalancing, AUC/A growth and NII are all working together.

The stock can continue to rerate if the dual guide upgrade is delivered and expense growth remains tied to revenue rather than structural cost creep. If markets calm abruptly and costs stay high, the operating leverage story weakens.

PM read: STT and BNY together confirm that custody banks are in a structural beat cycle for now.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

Shares rose after the release as investors rewarded the top-line beat, adjusted EPS beat and dual guide upgrade.

Peer read-across: strongly positive for BNY Mellon and other asset-servicing platforms. It also supports the broader theme that elevated macro/geopolitical volatility can be revenue-accretive for institutional market infrastructure businesses.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

Fee revenue guide durability
Why it matters: It determines whether Q1 was structural or mostly volatility-driven.

Expense growth
Why it matters: Higher cost guidance is the main offset to the revenue upgrade.

FX trading activity
Why it matters: FX volatility was a direct earnings driver and may normalize if markets calm.

BOTTOM LINE

Strong quarter.
State Street confirmed the custodian-bank beat cycle and upgraded both fee and NII guidance.
The next issue is whether higher expense growth stays proportional to revenue or starts to dilute operating leverage.

| Market reaction | Positive | — | Stock up strongly | — | — | Market rewarded credit improvement and NIM guide confidence. |

SECTOR KPI TABLE

| KPI | Actual | Prior / Benchmark | Change | PM Comment |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| NIM ex-OID | 3.52% | FY guide 3.60%-3.70% | Tracking | Mechanical expansion path intact. |
| FY NIM guide | 3.60%-3.70% | Prior guide | Reaffirmed | Key bull-case anchor. |
| Adjusted net revenue | $2.2B | Q1 2025 | +6% YoY | +12% excluding credit card sale. |

OFFICIAL EARNINGS DETAIL

Revenue / Sales:
Adjusted net revenue was $2.2B, up 6% YoY and up 12% excluding the credit card sale. Net financing revenue ex-OID was $1.6B, up 8% YoY.

Consensus:
GAAP EPS consensus was approximately $0.90, and reported EPS of $0.93 beat by $0.03.

EPS / Earnings:
Reported EPS was $0.93. Secondary summaries also reference adjusted EPS around $1.11, but the operative official headline used here is the GAAP EPS beat.

Profitability / Margins:
NIM ex-OID was 3.52%. FY2026 NIM guidance was reiterated at 3.60%-3.70%, with management expecting an exit rate at or above the high end of the range. CET1 was 10.1%.

Key KPI:
Retail auto net charge-offs were 1.97%, down 15bps YoY. Consolidated net charge-offs were 121bps, down 13bps QoQ and 29bps YoY. Record consumer auto loan applications point to sustained demand.

Guidance headline:
FY2026 NIM guidance was reiterated at 3.60%-3.70% under a no-Fed-cut assumption. Retail auto NCO guidance remains 1.8%-2.0%, and consolidated NCO guidance remains 1.2%-1.4%.

Capital allocation:
Capital remains adequate, with CET1 at 10.1%. The capital story is secondary to credit and NIM in this print.

Other relevant official details:
The funding-cost tailwind is mechanical: $18B of 2026 CD maturities carry a weighted-average yield near 4%, creating scope for repricing lower. Ally also reported a small plug-in hybrid lease termination loss, but it does not change the thesis.

SEGMENT / BUSINESS BREAKDOWN

SEGMENT TABLE

| Segment | % of Total | Revenue | YoY Growth | Margin Trend | Key Commentary |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| Auto Finance | Core | Not confirmed | Applications record | Improving | Credit quality improved despite sector concerns. |
| Deposits / Funding | Core funding source | $146B retail deposits | Stable | Improving | CD repricing provides mechanical NIM support. |
| Corporate Finance / Other | Smaller | Not confirmed | Not confirmed | Stable | Not central to Q1 thesis. |
| NIM / Balance Sheet | Not meaningful | $1.6B net financing revenue ex-OID | +8% | Improving | Key profitability lever. |

COMMENTARY

The quarter was important because it moved directly against the auto-credit bear case. At a time when national auto credit stress remains a market concern, Ally showed improving delinquencies, lower retail auto NCOs and better consolidated charge-offs.

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

The NIM guide reiteration is the main forward signal. Management is not relying on Fed cuts to achieve the 3.60%-3.70% NIM range; the mechanical driver is deposit repricing and CD maturities.

Capital is adequate, but the equity story is not about capital return. It is about whether credit and NIM can normalize enough to support mid-teens ROTCE.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

The professional tape framed the quarter as clean and thesis-validating. RBC commentary described the quarter as solid, with margin expansion, expense control and clean credit.

American Banker highlighted the counter-trend nature of Ally’s delinquency improvement relative to broader U.S. auto-loan stress. That is the key narrative point: Ally is not simply moving with the sector; it is showing differentiated underwriting and portfolio seasoning.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality part of the print was credit. Retail auto NCOs fell YoY, consolidated NCOs declined both YoY and QoQ, and 60-plus-day delinquencies improved.

The second-quality signal was NIM. The 3.60%-3.70% guide is supported by CD repricing, not by a heroic rate-cut assumption.

The main caveat is macro sensitivity. Auto finance remains exposed to employment, used-car prices, energy prices and lower-end consumer stress.

State transition: Improvement

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether Ally’s auto-credit book would deteriorate with broader consumer stress. Q1 pushes back against that bear case with improving delinquencies, lower NCOs and disciplined originations. The thesis improves, but needs another quarter of confirmation.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

Ally is a credit-inflection trade. The stock reaction was strong because the print addressed the exact bearish concern: auto credit did not deteriorate; it improved.

The rerating can continue if Q2 confirms lower delinquencies, stable used-car residuals and NIM expansion toward the high end of guidance. The risk is that macro pressure reappears through job losses, energy prices or used-car price weakness.

For PM purposes, Ally is the most direct signal that disciplined consumer auto lenders can outperform the broader auto-credit stress narrative.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

The stock rose strongly after the print as investors priced the credit inflection and NIM guide confidence.

Peer read-across: positive for higher-quality consumer finance lenders and auto lenders with disciplined underwriting; less positive for lower-credit-quality exposures where national delinquency stress remains unresolved.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

Delinquency trend
Why it matters: This is the central bear-case test.

NIM expansion
Why it matters: The 3.60%-3.70% guide is the path toward mid-teens ROTCE.

Used-car and EV / hybrid residual values
Why it matters: Lease residual pressure could become more material if used-vehicle prices weaken.

BOTTOM LINE

Strong thesis-validation quarter.
Ally delivered cleaner credit and reiterated a mechanical NIM expansion path.
The next issue is whether Q2 confirms that the auto-credit improvement is durable rather than a one-quarter relief print.

<<ALLY_EARNINGS_END>>

Simandou ramp

Pilbara recovery from cyclone disruption
Why it matters: Shipment recovery is needed to keep FY guidance credible.

China demand
Why it matters: Iron ore and copper placement still depend on Chinese industrial demand.

BOTTOM LINE

Solid continuation update.

Revenue / Sales:
Revenue was $111.7B, up 2% YoY.

Consensus:
Consensus was approximately $109.6B revenue and $6.57 EPS. UNH beat both.

EPS / Earnings:
GAAP EPS was $6.90 and adjusted EPS was $7.23.

Profitability / Margins:
MCR was 83.9%, improved from 84.8% in Q1 2025. Operating cash flow was $8.9B, or 1.4x net income.

Key KPI:
Domestic members declined to 49.1M from 49.8M at year-end 2025, reflecting intentional right-sizing rather than failed growth.

Guidance headline:
FY adjusted EPS guidance was raised to above $18.25 from above $17.75. Revenue guidance remained above $439B.

Capital allocation:
UNH expects at least $2B of share repurchase by the end of Q2.

| Optum Rx / Other Optum | Not confirmed | Not confirmed | Positive | Stable | Not the core debate this quarter. |
| Corporate / Eliminations | Not meaningful | Not confirmed | Not meaningful | Not meaningful | Not thesis-driving. |

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

The guide raise matters because it converts the MCR improvement from a one-quarter datapoint into management confidence. UNH is not chasing unprofitable enrollment; it is shrinking to repair earnings quality.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

The professional tape framed the print as a turnaround gaining traction. MCR was below feared levels, Optum improved, and guidance moved up.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality signal is MCR. After two years of cost pressure, a 90bps YoY improvement changes the debate.

The lowest-quality risk is durability. One quarter does not end the managed-care utilization debate. Q2 must prove that pricing and right-sizing are not just a temporary margin patch.

State transition: Reversal

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether UNH could repair MCR and Optum Health after a long earnings compression cycle. Q1 provides the first clean evidence that the repair is working. The thesis shifts from damage control to margin recovery.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

This is the week’s most important healthcare reversal. The market was not buying growth; it was buying proof that the managed-care earnings base stopped deteriorating.

If MCR holds and Optum keeps recovering, UNH can rerate from crisis multiple toward compounder multiple. If utilization reaccelerates, the rally reverses fast.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

Shares rose sharply after the print. Read-across is positive for managed care where pricing discipline and membership right-sizing can offset utilization pressure.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

MCR durability
Why it matters: This is the core turnaround datapoint.

Optum Health margin
Why it matters: Optum was the crisis pocket; recovery must persist.

Membership right-sizing
Why it matters: Shrinking unprofitable membership is constructive only if earnings improve.

BOTTOM LINE

True reversal print.
UNH finally showed margin repair where the market needed it most.
The next issue is whether Q2 confirms that the MCR turn is sustainable.

THEMES: Aerospace spin, automation, Middle East headwind, orders, backlog, segment margin, portfolio simplification
SEGMENT_TABLE_STATUS: Full
SOURCE_ROLE: Full note

EXEC SUMMARY

Stabilization. Honeywell delivered adjusted EPS growth of 11%, segment margin expansion and organic orders up 7%.
The stock sold off because revenue missed, Q2 guidance disappointed, and Middle East disruption hit Process & Automation.
PM read: underlying execution is better than the price action, but the June 29 Aerospace spin is the real rerating event.

Profitability / Margins:
GAAP operating margin was 16.1%, while segment margin was 23.3%, up 90bps.

Key KPI:
Organic orders rose 7%, and backlog reached $38.3B.

Guidance headline:
FY2026 guidance was maintained, but Q2 guidance was below consensus and includes tax / revenue headwinds.

Capital allocation:
The key capital / corporate action is separation. Aerospace spin is planned for June 29, 2026, and WWS is being sold.

The lowest-quality issue is the Q2 bridge: Middle East revenue headwind plus tax headwind delays the clean separation story.

The prior debate was whether Honeywell could create value through portfolio simplification. Q1 does not invalidate that. It adds near-term noise before the Aerospace spin.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

HON is a catalyst story disguised as a messy quarter. The stock can stay under pressure until Q2 clears, but June 29 creates a hard calendar for valuation separation.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

The stock sold off after the print. Read-across is negative for industrials with Middle East exposure, but positive for aerospace pure-play separation logic.

Aerospace spin execution
Why it matters: This is the primary rerating catalyst.

Middle East revenue recovery
Why it matters: The Q1/Q2 headwind must not become structural.

Segment margin durability
Why it matters: Margin execution is the main offset to slow revenue.

BOTTOM LINE

Noisy stabilization quarter.
The business is not broken, but Q2 guide and Middle East disruption dominate the tape.
The next issue is whether the Aerospace spin unlocks value after the near-term guide noise clears.

The caveat is spending. Higher OpEx and CapEx mean the turnaround is not yet a clean FCF story.

The professional tape framed the print as validation of Lip-Bu Tan’s turnaround. The after-hours move reflected both low starting expectations and real DCAI momentum.

The highest-quality signal is DCAI +22%. Intel is regaining data-center relevance where the market had written it off.
The lowest-quality caveat is foundry. External foundry revenue remains tiny versus the ambition.

State transition: Reversal

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether Intel’s decline was structural and irreversible. Q1 challenges that narrative. The thesis shifts from terminal decline toward product-led turnaround, while foundry remains optionality.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

Intel is a reversal trade, not yet a clean compounder. The stock moved because expectations were depressed and Q2 guidance shocked the model.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

Shares jumped after-hours. Read-across is positive for AI server CPU demand and data-center semi recovery, but not necessarily for foundry peers.

DCAI momentum

Gross margin sustainability
Why it matters: Q1 benefited from favorable mix / inventory; Q2 margin falls to 39%.

Foundry customer traction
Why it matters: The long-term thesis requires external foundry wins.

BOTTOM LINE

True reversal print.

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

Guidance is supportive but should not be overread. Q1 organic growth was flattered by six extra days and concentrate shipment timing, while FY organic growth remains 4%-5%.

The EPS guide raise is positive, but partly tax-driven. That does not undermine the quarter, but it means operating volume and share gains matter more than the headline EPS upgrade.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

The professional tape framed the quarter as clean execution: volume positive, price/mix positive, EPS beat and guidance raised. The only caveat was the calendar / concentrate timing boost to organic revenue.

The highest-quality signal is volume. In staples, +3% unit case volume is more valuable than pricing-only growth.
The lowest-quality issue is the optics of +10% organic growth. It is not the true run-rate because calendar and concentrate timing inflated Q1.

State transition: Continuation

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether staples could keep volume while consumer price sensitivity rose. Coca-Cola answered positively. The story remains a disciplined global compounder rather than a new acceleration regime.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

KO is a clean defensive quality print. It does not require a heroic macro view: volume, share and margin are all moving correctly.

The stock’s upside is likely steady rather than explosive. The main PM use is confirmation that premium global staples with brand power can still compound through a volatile consumer backdrop.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

Market reaction was constructive. Read-across is positive for global branded staples with pricing power and volume resilience, but less useful for weaker food / beverage names dependent on pricing alone.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

Underlying volume
Why it matters: Q1 organic growth was timing-aided; volume is the cleaner demand measure.

Tax-aided EPS guide raise
Why it matters: EPS guidance improved partly because of tax, not only operations.

Commodity costs
Why it matters: Management flagged volatility in inputs such as tea; margins need continued discipline.

BOTTOM LINE

Clean continuation quarter.
Coca-Cola delivered volume, share, margin and EPS guidance.
The next issue is whether volume growth persists after Q1 calendar / concentrate timing normalizes.

Profitability was supported by resilient core auto operations and a $500M tariff refund benefit. EV realignment charges of $1.1B weighed on the GAAP narrative.

OnStar revenue exceeded $750M, up 20% YoY. Super Cruise revenue rose 85% YoY and passed 1B miles driven.

Guidance headline:
FY EBIT-adjusted guidance was raised to $13.5B-$15.5B. Adjusted EPS guidance was raised to $11.50-$13.50. Automotive FCF guidance remained $9B-$11B.

Capital allocation:
The central capital-allocation message is discipline: EV capacity is being right-sized, while software / services revenue becomes more visible.

SEGMENT / BUSINESS BREAKDOWN

SEGMENT TABLE

| Segment | % of Total | Revenue | YoY Growth | Margin Trend | Key Commentary |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| North America Auto | Core | Not confirmed | Stable | Resilient | Main earnings engine. |
| EV / Capacity Realignment | Strategic drag | Not confirmed | Charges | Improving discipline | $1.1B special charges reflect right-sizing. |
| Software / OnStar | Growing | >$750M | +20% | High quality | Visible recurring revenue. |
| Super Cruise | Emerging | Not confirmed | +85% revenue | Positive | Monetization proof. |
| China | Smaller but relevant | Not confirmed | Profitable | Stable | Premium focus supports profitability. |

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

The guide raise is important because it says tariffs did not create the bear-case earnings collapse. Lower gross tariff-cost assumptions and maintained FCF guidance support the stock.

The EV charge is not a thesis break. It is a strategic reset toward capital discipline, which the market is likely to reward if losses keep declining.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

The professional tape framed GM as a resilience story: large EPS beat, guidance raise, tariff relief and software monetization, partly offset by EV charges and cost headwinds.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality signal is that even stripping out the tariff refund, underlying earnings were better than feared.
The lowest-quality issue is EPS cleanliness. The $500M tariff refund and EV charges mean the beat is not a simple clean operating beat.

State transition: Stabilization

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether tariffs and EV losses would overwhelm GM’s earnings base. Q1 weakens that bear case. The business is stabilizing, but the EV transition still requires disciplined capital allocation.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

GM is becoming more investable as a disciplined legacy OEM with software optionality, not as an aggressive EV growth story. The rerating case depends on durable FCF and reduced EV drag.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

Shares reacted positively. Read-across is constructive for legacy OEMs that can absorb tariff volatility and right-size EV spending.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

Tariff cost evolution
Why it matters: The guide raise depends partly on lower tariff assumptions.

EV loss reduction
Why it matters: EV capital discipline is central to the equity story.

OnStar / Super Cruise revenue
Why it matters: Software monetization can improve GM’s multiple if it scales.

BOTTOM LINE

Resilient quarter, not perfectly clean.
GM beat hard and raised guidance, but tariff refunds and EV charges matter for quality.
The next issue is whether software growth and EV cost control sustain the improved guide.

OFFICIAL EARNINGS DETAIL

Revenue / Sales:
Consolidated revenue was $21.2B, down 1.6% YoY but above consensus. U.S. Domestic revenue declined 2.3%, International revenue rose 3.8%, and Supply Chain Solutions revenue was $2.54B.

Consensus:
Revenue consensus was approximately $20.99B and adjusted EPS consensus approximately $1.02. UPS beat both.

EPS / Earnings:
Adjusted EPS was $1.07.

Profitability / Margins:
U.S. Domestic operating margin was 3.6%, International margin was 12.0%, and Supply Chain Solutions margin was 8.1%.

Key KPI:
UPS reduced non-lucrative Amazon volume by roughly 500,000 pieces per day on average and closed 23 additional facilities. SMB penetration reached 34.5% of total U.S. volume.

Guidance headline:
Full-year revenue guidance of $89.7B and non-GAAP adjusted operating margin of 9.6% were reaffirmed. Q2 U.S. Domestic operating margin is guided to 7.5%-8.5%.

Capital allocation:
The capital story is operational transformation, not buyback surprise. $600M of Q1 cost savings supports the $3B FY efficiency target.

SEGMENT / BUSINESS BREAKDOWN

SEGMENT TABLE

| Segment | % of Total | Revenue | YoY Growth | Margin Trend | Key Commentary |

| International Package | 21% | $4.54B | +3.8% | Strong | Best profitability at 12% margin. |
| Supply Chain Solutions | 12% | $2.54B | Not confirmed | Stable | 8.1% margin supports mix. |
| SMB Volume | Not segment | Not meaningful | +1.6% daily volume | Positive | Higher-quality replacement volume. |

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

The guidance hold is supportive but not enough. Investors need to see Q2 Domestic margin move toward 7.5%-8.5% to validate the transformation.

The Amazon glide-down is strategically correct. It reduces low-margin volume, but it creates a near-term optics problem until SMB and network savings offset the lost density.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

The professional tape focused on the contradiction: UPS beat the quarter but sold off because investors were not convinced by the margin bridge.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality signal is mix improvement: SMB share is rising while non-lucrative Amazon volume is being reduced.
The lowest-quality issue is U.S. Domestic margin. At 3.6%, it is too weak for the market to underwrite the recovery without Q2 confirmation.

State transition: Stabilization

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether UPS could execute its “better, not bigger” strategy without damaging earnings. Q1 keeps the thesis alive but does not prove it. Q2 margin delivery is now the verification point.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

UPS is a transformation trade. The stock will not work simply because management says it is exiting bad volume; it works only when the margin bridge appears in reported numbers.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

Shares fell around 4%. Read-across is cautious for parcel / logistics names where mix improvement requires near-term volume pain.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

Q2 U.S. Domestic margin
Why it matters: This is the transformation proof point.

SMB penetration
Why it matters: Higher-margin SMB volume must replace low-quality Amazon volume.

Cost savings delivery
Why it matters: The $3B savings target is the bridge to margin recovery.

BOTTOM LINE

Strategically right, tactically unproven.
UPS is improving mix, but the margin proof is still ahead.
The next issue is whether Q2 Domestic margin confirms the transformation.

<<V_EARNINGS_BEGIN>>
TICKER: V
COMPANY: Visa Inc.
SECTOR: Financials
SUBSECTOR: Payments
COUNTRY: United States
PUBLICATION_DATE: 2026-04-28
EVENT: Q2 FY2026
STATE_TRANSITION: Continuation
IMPORTANCE: ****
MARKET_REACTION: Mixed
MARKET_REACTION_DETAIL: Shares were mixed as strong Q2 revenue, cross-border and EPS growth were partly offset by April cross-border moderation and tax-related EPS quality debate.
PM_READ: Visa remains a top-quality global payments compounder, but the market is watching April cross-border moderation and tax-aided EPS upside.
THEMES: Payments, cross-border, value-added services, stablecoins, Agent Pay, tax benefit, premium consumer, processed transactions
SEGMENT_TABLE_STATUS: Partial
SOURCE_ROLE: Full note

EXEC SUMMARY

Continuation. Visa delivered 17% net revenue growth, 20% non-GAAP EPS growth, 8% payment volume growth and 11% cross-border ex-intra-Europe growth.
The core signal remains strong global payments activity, especially cross-border and value-added services.
PM read: the business is at the top of its cycle, but April moderation and lower tax rate should temper EPS extrapolation.

HEADLINE EARNINGS TABLE

| Metric | Actual | Consensus / Prior | Beat / Miss | YoY | QoQ | PM Comment |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|
| Revenue / Sales | $11.2B net revenue | Consensus not confirmed | — | +17%; +16% constant-dollar | — | Fast growth for a mature payments network. |
| EPS / Earnings | $3.14 GAAP EPS; $3.31 non-GAAP EPS | Consensus not confirmed | — | GAAP +36%; non-GAAP +20% | — | Strong EPS, partly helped by tax-rate benefit. |
| Profitability / Margin | GAAP net income $6.0B | Prior year lower | — | Strong | — | High-quality profitability. |
| Guidance / Outlook | FY revenue low double digits; EPS low double digits at higher end due tax | Prior guide | EPS tone improved | — | — | Tax benefit boosts EPS quality less than operations. |
| Market reaction | Mixed | — | Mixed | — | — | April cross-border moderation capped reaction. |

SECTOR KPI TABLE

| KPI | Actual | Prior / Benchmark | Change | PM Comment |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| Net revenue | $11.2B | Q2 FY2025 | +17% YoY | Very strong. |
| Non-GAAP EPS | $3.31 | Q2 FY2025 | +20% YoY | Strong but tax-aided. |
| GAAP EPS | $3.14 | Q2 FY2025 | +36% YoY | Legal/tax effects help. |
| Payments volume | $4.0T | Prior year | +8% cc | Solid core volume. |
| Cross-border ex-intra-Europe | +11% cc | Prior year | Strong | High-margin engine. |
| Processed transactions | 69B | Prior year | +9% YoY | Network activity healthy. |
| Value-added services revenue | +17% | Prior year | Strong | Higher-multiple growth driver. |
| FY tax rate | 18%-18.5% | Prior higher | Lower | EPS-quality caveat. |
| Stablecoin / Agent Pay | Development ongoing | — | Optionality | Medium-term rails expansion. |

OFFICIAL EARNINGS DETAIL

Revenue / Sales:
Net revenue was $11.2B, up 17% YoY and 16% on a constant-dollar basis.

Consensus:
Consensus not confirmed in the provided scout.

EPS / Earnings:
GAAP net income was $6.0B. GAAP EPS was $3.14, up 36%. Non-GAAP EPS was $3.31, up 20%.

Profitability / Margins:
Visa remains a high-margin payments network. EPS growth was supported by operating performance and a lower tax-rate outlook from legal settlement benefits.

Key KPI:
Payments volume was $4T, up 8% constant-dollar. Cross-border volume excluding intra-Europe rose 11%. Processed transactions rose 9% to 69B.

Guidance headline:
FY revenue growth guidance remains low double digits. EPS growth guidance is low double digits, with higher-end outcome helped by tax rate benefit.

Capital allocation:
Capital allocation was not the key issue. The strategic optionality is stablecoin and Agent Pay development.

SEGMENT / BUSINESS BREAKDOWN

SEGMENT TABLE

| Segment | % of Total | Revenue | YoY Growth | Margin Trend | Key Commentary |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| Payments Volume | Core driver | $4.0T volume | +8% cc | Strong | Consumer / commercial activity healthy. |
| Cross-Border | High margin | Not confirmed | +11% cc ex-intra-Europe | Strong | Main profit driver. |
| Processed Transactions | Core | 69B | +9% | Stable | Network usage healthy. |
| Value-Added Services | Growth | Not confirmed | +17% | Strong | Diversifies beyond interchange. |
| Stablecoin / Agent Pay | Optionality | Not meaningful | Emerging | Not meaningful | Medium-term payments-rail evolution. |

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

Guidance is steady, with EPS biased to the higher end because of tax rate benefit. That is positive, but not as high-quality as a pure operating raise.

April cross-border moderation is the near-term watch item. It does not break the thesis but matters for market reaction.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

The professional tape emphasized strong Q2 fundamentals and value-added services growth, while April cross-border softness created a near-term overhang.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality signal is cross-border growth. This remains Visa’s high-margin growth engine.
The lowest-quality issue is EPS quality. Lower tax rate boosts EPS and should not be capitalized like operating acceleration.

State transition: Continuation

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether payments volumes could remain resilient despite macro volatility. Q2 says yes. The only new caution is April cross-border softness.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

Visa remains one of the cleanest global consumption and travel rails. The tactical issue is whether April softness is noise or the first sign of geopolitical travel drag.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

Market reaction was mixed. Read-across is positive for payments and premium consumer activity, but watch Mastercard’s April cross-border commentary for confirmation.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

April cross-border trend
Why it matters: Cross-border is the highest-margin growth engine.

Tax-rate benefit
Why it matters: EPS growth is helped by non-operating / legal settlement effects.

Value-added services
Why it matters: Higher-growth services support the multiple.

BOTTOM LINE

Strong continuation quarter.
Visa is executing at a high level, but EPS is partly tax-aided and April cross-border needs monitoring.
The next issue is whether cross-border activity stays resilient through geopolitical volatility.

<<V_EARNINGS_END>>

<<SBUX_EARNINGS_BEGIN>>
TICKER: SBUX
COMPANY: Starbucks Corporation
SECTOR: Consumer Discretionary
SUBSECTOR: Restaurants
COUNTRY: United States
PUBLICATION_DATE: 2026-04-28
EVENT: Q2 FY2026
STATE_TRANSITION: Reversal
IMPORTANCE: ****
MARKET_REACTION: Positive
MARKET_REACTION_DETAIL: Shares rose sharply after-hours as Starbucks delivered traffic-led U.S. comp growth, EPS beat and a material same-store sales guidance raise.
PM_READ: The Niccol turnaround moved from narrative to evidence: U.S. traffic is positive, margins expanded, and guidance was raised materially.
THEMES: Restaurants, turnaround, traffic recovery, Back to Starbucks, U.S. comps, China, international comps, margin expansion, guidance raise
SEGMENT_TABLE_STATUS: Full
SOURCE_ROLE: Full note

EXEC SUMMARY

Reversal. Starbucks delivered global comps of +6.2%, U.S. comps of +7.1%, and non-GAAP EPS of $0.50.
The quality is high because U.S. comps were transaction-led, not just ticket-led.
PM read: this is the first definitive proof that the “Back to Starbucks” turnaround is working.

HEADLINE EARNINGS TABLE

| Metric | Actual | Consensus / Prior | Beat / Miss | YoY | QoQ | PM Comment |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|
| Revenue / Sales | $9.53B revenue | ~$9.17B consensus | Beat | +9% | — | Strong top-line recovery. |
| EPS / Earnings | $0.45 GAAP EPS; $0.50 non-GAAP EPS | ~$0.42-$0.44 consensus | Beat | GAAP +32%; non-GAAP +22% | — | Clean earnings beat. |
| Profitability / Margin | Operating margin 9.4% | Prior lower | Better | +110bps | — | Margin recovery confirms leverage. |
| Guidance / Outlook | Global and U.S. SSS at least +5%; non-GAAP EPS $2.25-$2.45 | Prior SSS +3% | Raised materially | — | — | Turnaround guide reset. |
| Market reaction | Positive | — | Stock +5%-6% AH | — | — | Market repriced turnaround credibility. |

SECTOR KPI TABLE

| KPI | Actual | Prior / Benchmark | Change | PM Comment |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| Global comparable sales | +6.2% | Prior weaker | Strong | Turnaround visible. |
| Global transactions | +3.8% | Prior period | Positive | Traffic-led recovery. |
| Global ticket | +2.3% | Prior period | Positive | Balanced comp. |
| U.S. comparable sales | +7.1% | Prior weaker | Strong | Core proof point. |
| U.S. transactions | +4.3% | Prior period | Strong | High-quality comp. |
| U.S. ticket | +2.7% | Prior period | Positive | Pricing / mix supportive. |
| International comps | +2.6% | Prior period | Positive | Broad recovery. |
| China comps | +0.5% | Prior negative context | Stabilizing | China no longer major drag. |
| Operating margin | 9.4% | Prior year | +110bps | Leverage returning. |
| FY SSS guide | At least +5% | Prior +3% | Raised | Major confidence signal. |
| Net new stores | 600-650 planned | Prior plan | Maintained | Growth investment continues. |

OFFICIAL EARNINGS DETAIL

Revenue / Sales:
Revenue was $9.53B, up 9% YoY and ahead of consensus.

Consensus:
Revenue consensus was around $9.17B. Non-GAAP EPS consensus was approximately $0.42-$0.44. Starbucks beat both.

EPS / Earnings:
GAAP EPS was $0.45, up 32% YoY. Non-GAAP EPS was $0.50, up 22% YoY.

Profitability / Margins:
Operating margin expanded 110bps to 9.4%.

Key KPI:
Global comparable sales rose 6.2%, driven by 3.8% transactions and 2.3% ticket. U.S. comparable sales rose 7.1%, driven by 4.3% transactions and 2.7% ticket.

Guidance headline:
FY global and U.S. same-store sales guidance was raised to at least 5%, from 3%. Non-GAAP EPS guidance is $2.25-$2.45.

Capital allocation:
New store growth remains part of the plan, with 600-650 net new stores expected for FY2026.

SEGMENT / BUSINESS BREAKDOWN

SEGMENT TABLE

| Segment | % of Total | Revenue | YoY Growth | Margin Trend | Key Commentary |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| U.S. / North America | Core | Not confirmed | Comps +7.1% | Improving | Traffic +4.3%; turnaround proof. |
| International | Material | Not confirmed | Comps +2.6% | Stabilizing | All top 10 international markets positive. |
| China | Key market | Not confirmed | Comps +0.5% | Stabilizing | No longer main drag. |
| Total Company | 100% | $9.53B | +9%; comps +6.2% | Margin +110bps | First simultaneous top and bottom-line growth in 2+ years. |

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

The guide raise is the key catalyst. Starbucks moved from a speculative turnaround to a verified turnaround because management raised same-store sales expectations materially and April trends remained positive.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

The professional tape focused on traffic. The market did not just reward a sales beat; it rewarded the fact that the U.S. comp was transaction-led.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality signal is U.S. transaction growth. Restaurants rerate when traffic returns, not when ticket carries comps.
The lowest-quality issue is China. +0.5% is stabilization, not a full China recovery.

State transition: Reversal

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether Brian Niccol’s “Back to Starbucks” plan could reverse traffic declines. Q2 answers yes. The debate now shifts from credibility to durability.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

This is the quarter where SBUX becomes an investable turnaround again. The stock can rerate if traffic remains positive and margins keep expanding.

The risk is that the market extrapolates too fast. One strong quarter validates the plan, but repetition is needed.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

Shares rose sharply after-hours. Read-across is positive for restaurant turnarounds where traffic improves, but Starbucks-specific execution matters.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

U.S. transaction growth
Why it matters: Traffic is the central turnaround proof point.

April momentum
Why it matters: Confirms Q2 exit rate was not one-off.

China recovery
Why it matters: Stabilization is helpful, but full recovery would add another leg.

BOTTOM LINE

True reversal quarter.
Starbucks traffic turned, margins expanded, and guidance was raised.
The next issue is whether Niccol can sustain this through the next two quarters.

PM_READ: Novartis is absorbing the Entresto cliff; new brands are accelerating, but the market still needs proof they can fully offset generic erosion.
THEMES: Pharma, Entresto generic erosion, priority brands, Kisqali, Pluvicto, Kesimpta, Scemblix, Avidity acquisition, leverage
SEGMENT_TABLE_STATUS: Partial
SOURCE_ROLE: Full note

EXEC SUMMARY

Stabilization. Novartis missed revenue and EPS consensus as Entresto and other mature brands faced severe generic erosion.
The new growth franchise is working: priority brands such as Kisqali, Pluvicto, Kesimpta, Scemblix and Leqvio grew strongly.
PM read: the transition is painful but intact; rerating requires new-brand growth to outpace the Entresto cliff.

OFFICIAL EARNINGS DETAIL

Revenue / Sales:
Net sales were $13.113B, down 1% reported and 5% constant currency.

Consensus:
Consensus was approximately $13.56B revenue and $2.11 core EPS. Novartis missed both.

EPS / Earnings:
Core EPS was $1.99, down 13% reported and 15% constant currency.

Profitability / Margins:
Core operating income declined 14% constant currency.

Key KPI:
Priority brands grew strongly, but Entresto, Promacta and Tasigna faced severe generic erosion.

Guidance headline:
FY guidance was maintained: low-single-digit net sales growth and low-single-digit core operating income decline at constant currency.

Capital allocation:
Net debt rose to $38.1B after the Avidity acquisition. Novartis repurchased $1.6B of shares in Q1.

SEGMENT / BUSINESS BREAKDOWN

SEGMENT TABLE

|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---|

| Kisqali | Major growth asset | $1.5B | +55% cc | Strong | Core breast cancer growth driver. |
| Pluvicto | Growth asset | $642M | +70% cc | Strong | Oncology growth. |
| Kesimpta | Growth asset | $1.2B | +26% cc | Strong | Neurology franchise. |
| Entresto / Mature Brands | Legacy cash engine | $1.3B Entresto | -46% cc | Weak | Generic erosion dominates headline. |

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

The guide hold matters because management views the erosion pattern as expected. Still, the market needs proof that new brands can offset the old cash-engine decline fast enough.

Avidity raises strategic optionality but increases leverage. Balance-sheet flexibility is now a watch item.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

The professional tape framed Q1 as a painful but expected transition: priority brands are doing their job, but Entresto erosion is still too large to ignore.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality signal is the breadth of priority-brand growth.
The lowest-quality issue is the speed of Entresto generic erosion. It is the binding constraint on headline growth.

State transition: Stabilization

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether Novartis could bridge the Entresto cliff without a major earnings reset. Q1 keeps the bridge intact but not yet comfortable.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

NVS is a transition stock. The growth assets are credible, but the market will not rerate until the cliff mathematically stops dominating the P&L.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

Shares traded negatively. Read-across is mixed for pharma: growth brands matter, but patent-cliff math still dominates.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

Entresto erosion pace
Why it matters: It determines how painful the transition is.

Priority-brand growth
Why it matters: Kisqali, Pluvicto, Kesimpta and Scemblix are the bridge.

Net debt after Avidity
Why it matters: Leverage constrains capital flexibility.

BOTTOM LINE

Stabilization, not improvement.
The new Novartis portfolio is growing, but the old cash engine is falling fast.
The next issue is whether priority brands can fully outrun the cliff by H2.

Reported profit attributable to shareholders was $3.8B. Underlying replacement cost profit was $3.2B versus $1.4B in Q1 2025.

Profitability / Margins:
Customers & Products RC profit was $3.2B, up sharply from $677M, driven by refining and oil trading.

Key KPI:
Net debt increased to $25.3B, up $3B in the quarter.

Guidance headline:
CapEx guidance was maintained at $13.0B-$13.5B. Buybacks remain suspended.

Capital allocation:
Buyback suspension is the key constraint. Asset disposals and cost cuts are now the route to balance-sheet credibility.

Professional commentary recognized that BP delivered better-than-expected operating results, but the buyback suspension and debt increase remain the equity overhang.

TICKER: GOOGL
COMPANY: Alphabet Inc.
SECTOR: Communication Services
SUBSECTOR: Search / Cloud / AI Infrastructure
COUNTRY: United States
PUBLICATION_DATE: 2026-04-29
EVENT: Q1 2026
STATE_TRANSITION: Continuation
IMPORTANCE: ****
MARKET_REACTION: Positive
MARKET_REACTION_DETAIL: Shares rose after-hours and rallied strongly the next session as investors rewarded Search resilience, Google Cloud acceleration, and backlog growth despite higher capex and equity-gain-inflated EPS.
PM_READ: Alphabet delivered the cleanest cloud-acceleration / Search-resilience print in the mega-cap AI batch, but reported EPS quality is distorted by a $36.9B equity-securities gain and future FCF still depends on capex conversion.
THEMES: Search resilience, Google Cloud acceleration, AI capex, Gemini, TPU, Cloud backlog, equity-security gains, EPS quality, FCF compression, AI Overviews, full-stack AI, enterprise AI demand
SEGMENT_TABLE_STATUS: Full
SOURCE_ROLE: Full note

EXEC SUMMARY

Continuation. Alphabet delivered a very strong operating print: consolidated revenue rose 22% YoY, or 19% constant currency, to $109.9B; operating income rose 30% to $39.7B; operating margin expanded by about 2 points to 36.1%; Google Search & other revenue rose 19%; and Google Cloud revenue accelerated 63% to $20.0B. The key operating message is that AI is not visibly cannibalizing Search monetization in this quarter, while Cloud demand is accelerating materially.

The market reaction was positive because Alphabet showed the clearest revenue offset to AI capex among the mega-cap internet / cloud names in the supplied batch. Cloud backlog nearly doubled quarter-on-quarter to more than $460B, enterprise AI solutions became the primary growth driver for Cloud, and management said cloud revenue would have been higher with more compute capacity. That framed the capex raise as a capacity-constrained growth investment rather than only a cash-flow drag.

The caveat is EPS quality. GAAP EPS was $5.11 and net income was $62.6B, but other income included a $37.7B net gain, primarily from unrealized gains on non-marketable equity securities; the gain on equity securities increased diluted EPS by $2.35. The clean PM read should therefore emphasize Search, Cloud revenue, Cloud backlog, operating income, Cloud operating margin, capex, and FCF rather than capitalizing the full reported EPS beat.
