OFFICIAL EARNINGS DETAIL

Revenue / Sales:
Strategy reported Q1 revenue of $124.3M, up 11.9% YoY and marginally below the roughly $125.1M forecast in the scout. The enterprise software business is stable but economically secondary to the Bitcoin treasury and digital-credit funding structure.

Consensus:
Consensus revenue was approximately $125.1M. Four analysts’ average GAAP EPS estimate was a loss of roughly $18.98 per share, with the most bearish forecast around -$36.89. Actual diluted EPS loss of $38.25 was worse than even that bear floor.

EPS / Earnings:
Net loss was $12.54B, or $38.25 per diluted share. Operating loss was $14.47B and included a $14.46B unrealized loss on digital assets. The loss is a fair-value accounting result tied to Bitcoin’s quarter-end price decline, not a cash operating loss from the software business.

Profitability / Margins:
Gross profit was $83.4M, representing 67.1% gross margin, versus 69.4% last year. GAAP operating margin is not analytically useful because the Bitcoin fair-value markdown overwhelms software economics.

Key KPI:
The key KPI is Bitcoin per share / BTC accretion, not GAAP EPS. Strategy held 818,334 BTC as of May 3, achieved 9.4% BTC Yield YTD, and reported 63,410 BTC Gain YTD.

Guidance headline:
No traditional earnings guidance is relevant. The forward guide is effectively capital-market access, BTC price, STRC dividend sustainability, and whether Strategy remains a net BTC accumulator without forced sales.

Capital allocation:
Strategy continued aggressive BTC accumulation, funded by equity and preferred issuance. The company raised $11.68B YTD and scaled STRC to a major preferred instrument. The structural capital-allocation issue is that preferred dividend obligations may require BTC sales if cash, ATM issuance, or BTC appreciation are insufficient.

ACCOUNTING / EPS QUALITY CHECK

Reported earnings quality:
Distorted

Adjustment issue:
The $12.54B net loss was driven by a $14.46B unrealized loss on Bitcoin holdings under ASC 350-60 fair-value accounting. This is a non-cash mark-to-market item. No operating collapse in the software business caused the loss. No material tax benefit, acquisition gain, tariff refund, litigation settlement, discontinued-operation gain, derivative timing gain, reserve release, or impairment reversal was identified as the operative item.

PM interpretation:
Ignore GAAP EPS as the primary performance metric. Use BTC holdings, BTC per share / BTC Yield, NAV premium, capital raised, cash coverage, preferred dividend obligations, and potential BTC-sale risk as the clean investment bridge. The GAAP loss is accounting noise; the “may sell Bitcoin to fund dividends” commentary is the real thesis change.

SEGMENT / BUSINESS BREAKDOWN

SEGMENT TABLE

| Segment | % of Total | Revenue | YoY Growth | Margin Trend | Key Commentary |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| Bitcoin treasury | Economic majority | Not revenue-generating | BTC holdings +22% YTD | NAV-driven | Core equity driver; 818,334 BTC held. |
| Enterprise software | Accounting revenue base | $124.3M total company revenue | +11.9% YoY | Gross margin 67.1% | Stable but no longer central to valuation. |
| Digital Credit / STRC | Financing layer | Not operating revenue | STRC proceeds +189% YTD | Dividend obligation rising | Preferred structure funds BTC accumulation but creates senior claims. |
| ATM equity issuance | Financing layer | $11.68B YTD raised | Large issuance | Dilution-sensitive | Capital-market access remains central to BTC accumulation. |
| BTC fair-value accounting | Non-operating P&L driver | -$14.46B unrealized loss | Larger loss than prior year | Distorts GAAP P&L | Main reason GAAP EPS is unusable. |

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

Strategy’s effective guidance is not software revenue or GAAP EPS. It is whether the company can keep raising capital accretively, maintain preferred dividend obligations, grow BTC per share, and avoid forced BTC sales.

The new structural issue is that management commentary opened the door to selling Bitcoin to cover dividends. That is a fundamental change from the prior “never sell” doctrine. Bulls may frame it as prudent liability management and a small backstop; bears will frame it as proof that the preferred stack has created a structural claim on the Bitcoin treasury.

Revision direction: mix-shift revisions. GAAP EPS estimates will remain BTC-price driven and may reverse if BTC rebounds. But equity-risk assumptions should move more cautious because the preferred dividend structure and possible BTC-sale backstop change the capital-allocation framework.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

The professional tape correctly ignored the software business and focused on two items: the non-cash Bitcoin markdown and the “never sell” reversal. Reuters and company materials confirmed the massive GAAP loss and the large BTC holdings, while crypto-focused coverage highlighted that management commentary had opened the door to selling Bitcoin to fund dividends.

This matters because MSTR’s equity premium historically depended on the idea that the company is a perpetual BTC accumulator. If Bitcoin can be sold to service preferred obligations, the equity becomes less of a pure BTC accumulation vehicle and more of a leveraged capital-structure trade with senior claims.

The Twitter / X layer is split. Saylor highlighted BTC holdings, BTC Yield, BTC Gain, STRC scale and capital raised. Bears such as Peter Schiff focused on the preferred structure and dividend sustainability. Polymarket probability of BTC sales moved higher after the call, confirming that the market treated the commentary as a real state change.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality positive signal is continued capital-market access. Strategy raised $11.68B YTD and added BTC aggressively, which means the machine is still functioning.

The highest-quality negative signal is preferred dividend burden. STRC and the broader preferred stack introduce a recurring cash obligation that can force difficult choices if BTC underperforms.

The lowest-quality reported metric is GAAP EPS. It is mechanically driven by quarter-end BTC price and can reverse sharply in Q2 if Bitcoin rebounds.

The debate driving revisions is no longer only BTC NAV premium. It is whether MSTR deserves a BTC NAV premium when preferred dividends may require BTC sales. Analysts will likely keep NAV-based frameworks but apply a higher capital-structure discount.

State transition: Deterioration

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether Strategy could keep using capital markets to accretively grow BTC per share. Q1 confirms the capital machine still works, but it also introduces a new structural question: can preferred dividends be serviced without ever selling BTC. The investment thesis moved from pure accumulation toward accumulation with conditional liquidation risk.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

Print reaction function:
The market knew the GAAP loss would be ugly because BTC fell during Q1. The surprise was not the loss; it was the dividend-funding language. The reaction makes sense: an initial relief move on BTC holdings and capital raised reversed when investors understood that the “never sell” doctrine was no longer absolute. What needs to happen next is BTC appreciation, continued ATM access, stable STRC demand, enough cash / issuance to fund dividends, and no meaningful BTC sale. What would break the thesis is a sustained BTC drawdown, NAV premium compression, closed capital markets, or actual BTC liquidation to service preferreds. Peer read-across is negative for levered crypto-treasury vehicles using preferred or credit structures to finance accumulation.

Stock identity:
Optionality stock; setup-risk stock; crowded long; deep value not applicable; value trap risk; high-beta BTC proxy. The print challenges the pure leveraged-BTC identity because senior dividend obligations now matter. MSTR remains a BTC-upside vehicle, but it is no longer analytically clean if BTC sales are a real funding backstop.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

The stock reaction was mixed: an initial after-hours rise on BTC accumulation and capital-raising metrics reversed lower after management commentary suggested Bitcoin sales could be used to cover dividends.

Peer read-across is important for crypto-treasury vehicles. If preferred dividends and yield products become part of the crypto-treasury model, investors must analyze not just NAV premium and coin holdings, but also senior claims, dividend coverage, cash runway and forced-sale risk.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

BTC sales / Why it matters: Any actual sale would validate the structural thesis shift and pressure the NAV premium.

STRC dividend coverage / Why it matters: Preferred dividend obligations are now the main capital-structure stress point.

NAV premium / Why it matters: The entire issuance model depends on maintaining a premium to BTC NAV.

Capital-market access / Why it matters: Continued equity / preferred issuance allows BTC accumulation without asset sales.

Bitcoin price path / Why it matters: GAAP P&L, NAV, dividend coverage and investor sentiment are all BTC-price sensitive.

BOTTOM LINE

MSTR’s GAAP loss is mostly non-cash Bitcoin accounting noise.
The real story is the potential end of the absolute “never sell” doctrine.
BTC accumulation continues, but preferred dividends introduce structural funding pressure.
Deterioration: not because of software or GAAP EPS, but because the equity thesis is less pure.
PM focus: BTC sales, STRC dividend coverage, NAV premium and capital-market access.

<<MSTR_EARNINGS_END>>

TICKER: PFE

PUBLICATION_DATE: 2026-05-05
EVENT: Q1 2026
STATE_TRANSITION: Stabilization
IMPORTANCE: ***
MARKET_REACTION: Neutral
MARKET_REACTION_DETAIL: Shares faded to a modest decline after an initial positive reaction as investors acknowledged the Q1 beat but focused on reaffirmed rather than raised guidance and persistent LOE headwinds.
PM_READ: Pfizer’s non-COVID portfolio is stabilizing and the Vyndamax settlement improves the post-2028 bridge, but the lack of a guide raise keeps the near-term equity reaction muted.
THEMES: Non-COVID growth, launched and acquired products, Seagen, Padcev, Eliquis, Abrysvo, Comirnaty decline, Paxlovid decline, Vyndamax LOE, Belgian court ruling, 2029 CAGR, dividend support, post-pandemic reset
SEGMENT_TABLE_STATUS: Partial

EXEC SUMMARY

Stabilization. Pfizer reported Q1 revenue of $14.45B, up 5% YoY and above consensus around $13.84B. Adjusted EPS was $0.75 versus consensus around $0.72, while GAAP diluted EPS was $0.47, down 10% YoY. Non-COVID revenue grew 7% operationally and launched / acquired products grew 22% operationally.

The operating signal is better than the stock reaction suggests. Padcev, Eliquis, Abrysvo and the launched / acquired portfolio are offsetting the COVID cliff, while Comirnaty and Paxlovid remain large declines. The Vyndamax settlement is the most important non-quarterly development because it improves the 2029+ growth and cash-flow bridge.

PM read: this is a stabilization print, not a full re-rating print. Pfizer beat Q1 and improved medium-term visibility, but guidance was reaffirmed, not raised, and the market remains focused on 2025-2028 LOE pressure. The Vyndamax development matters more for post-2028 models than for the current stock tape.

OFFICIAL EARNINGS DETAIL

Revenue / Sales:
Pfizer reported Q1 revenue of $14.45B, up 5% YoY and above LSEG consensus around $13.84B. Non-COVID revenue rose 7% operationally, and launched / acquired products grew 22% operationally.

Consensus:
Revenue consensus was approximately $13.84B and adjusted EPS consensus approximately $0.72. Pfizer beat both, but FY guidance was reaffirmed rather than raised.

EPS / Earnings:
Adjusted EPS was $0.75, above consensus. GAAP diluted EPS was $0.47, down 10% YoY. Adjusted EPS declined YoY because of prior-year comparison effects, R&D investment, tax rate and cost structure, despite the Q1 beat.

Profitability / Margins:
Adjusted gross margin was 76%. Adjusted operating expenses were $5.5B, up 4% operationally, with adjusted R&D up 11% operationally. Cost control was acceptable, but Pfizer is still investing through the LOE bridge.

Key KPI:
The key KPI is non-COVID and launched / acquired product growth. Padcev, Eliquis and Abrysvo beat estimates, while Comirnaty and Paxlovid missed and remain the pandemic-cliff drag.

Guidance headline:
Pfizer reaffirmed FY2026 revenue guidance of $59.5B-$62.5B and adjusted EPS guidance of $2.80-$3.00. The guide embeds around $1.5B of unfavorable revenue impact from patent / regulatory losses.

Capital allocation:
Pfizer assumes no share repurchases in 2026 and diluted shares of around 5.74B. The Vyndamax settlement and Belgian court ruling improve confidence in future cash flow and dividend support, but the current year remains a transition period.

ACCOUNTING / EPS QUALITY CHECK

Reported earnings quality:
Mixed

Adjustment issue:
Adjusted EPS of $0.75 was above GAAP EPS of $0.47. The gap reflects restructuring charges, intangible amortization and other standard pharmaceutical adjustments. No material non-operating gain, tax benefit, acquisition gain, mark-to-market gain, unrealized equity-security gain, tariff refund, derivative timing gain, discontinued-operation gain, reserve release, or litigation settlement gain was identified as the driver of the Q1 beat.

PM interpretation:
Use adjusted EPS, non-COVID operational revenue, launched / acquired product growth, product-level beats / misses, R&D investment, LOE timing and FY guidance as the clean bridge. GAAP EPS is less useful because restructuring and amortization distort the operating read, but the adjusted beat should not be overcapitalized without a guide raise.

SEGMENT / BUSINESS BREAKDOWN

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

Pfizer reaffirmed FY2026 revenue guidance of $59.5B-$62.5B and adjusted EPS guidance of $2.80-$3.00. Reaffirmation is stabilizing, but the lack of a raise after a Q1 beat limited the stock reaction.

The structural positive is Vyndamax. The settlement delays LOE by roughly 2.5 years for a major product and supports management’s statement that starting in 2029 Pfizer can enter a five-year period of high-single-digit revenue CAGR. The Belgian Comirnaty ruling also supports future EPS and cash flow.

Revision direction: mix-shift revisions. Near-term FY2026 estimates may not move much because guidance was reaffirmed, but post-2028 / 2029+ revenue, EPS and cash-flow assumptions should improve because of the Vyndamax settlement and improved legal visibility.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

The professional tape was neutral-to-constructive. CNBC and other public coverage highlighted the Q1 beat and the strength in launched / acquired products. ChartMill and StockStory-type commentary focused on the lack of guidance raise and ongoing LOE headwinds as the reason for the muted stock reaction.

The market is not ignoring the beat; it is refusing to pay for it until Pfizer proves the bridge through 2028 is durable. That is why the Vyndamax settlement is more important than the Q1 EPS beat. It changes the post-2028 growth runway, but not enough to produce an immediate near-term guide raise.

The Twitter / X / professional layer was also neutral. Management framed the quarter as strong and pointed to non-COVID portfolio execution. The market framed it as “better, but not yet enough.” The stock reaction was therefore rational: the print stabilizes the thesis but does not yet force a re-rating.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality signal is launched / acquired product growth of 22% operationally. That is the proof point that Pfizer’s post-COVID portfolio is not standing still.

The second highest-quality signal is the Vyndamax settlement. It materially improves the post-2028 bridge and supports management’s 2029 high-single-digit CAGR language.

The lowest-quality signal is the COVID product line. Comirnaty and Paxlovid continue to miss and decline sharply, keeping the pandemic-cliff narrative alive.

The debate driving revisions is whether launched / acquired products plus Vyndamax LOE relief are sufficient to offset the 2025-2028 LOE gauntlet. Analysts may leave 2026 estimates broadly stable but raise outer-year confidence.

State transition: Stabilization

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether Pfizer’s post-pandemic portfolio could stabilize before the LOE cycle weighed further on growth. Q1 supports stabilization, with non-COVID revenue +7% operational and launched / acquired products +22%. The Vyndamax settlement improves the outer-year bridge, but the lack of guide raise keeps the near-term debate unresolved.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

Print reaction function:
The market was pricing Pfizer as a cheap, dividend-supported pharma name facing a known LOE gauntlet. The Q1 beat cleared the quarterly bar but did not force a 2026 guide raise, so the stock reaction was muted. What changed is the post-2028 bridge: Vyndamax LOE delay and legal clarity improve confidence in 2029+ growth. What needs to happen next is continued non-COVID growth, Padcev / Seagen execution, Abrysvo durability, pipeline milestone delivery, and no deterioration in LOE impact. What would break the thesis is launched / acquired product slowdown, pipeline disappointment, worse-than-guided LOE drag, or dividend pressure. Peer read-across is constructive for large pharma with credible LOE-bridge assets but neutral for the broader sector.

Stock identity:
Defensive compounder; yield / income stock; turnaround; value stock; setup-risk stock. The print reinforces the stabilization / income-stock identity rather than a high-growth identity. Pfizer is still a value / dividend recovery story, but the Vyndamax settlement reduces the risk that it becomes a value trap through the post-2028 window.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

Pfizer shares were modestly negative in the session after an initial positive reaction. The move was driven by the absence of a guide raise and the market’s continued focus on LOE headwinds, not by Q1 operational disappointment.

Peer read-across is positive for companies with credible acquired-product growth and legal / patent extensions that improve outer-year visibility. It is not a broad pharma re-rating signal because Pfizer remains in a company-specific post-COVID and LOE transition.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

Launched / acquired product growth / Why it matters: This is the core offset to COVID decline and LOE pressure.

Vyndamax LOE timing / Why it matters: The settlement materially improves the post-2028 cash-flow bridge.

COVID product decline / Why it matters: Comirnaty and Paxlovid misses still drag reported growth.

Pipeline milestones / Why it matters: Pfizer needs R&D conversion to support the 2029 high-single-digit CAGR claim.

Guidance raise potential / Why it matters: The stock likely needs upward revisions, not just reaffirmation, to rerate.

BOTTOM LINE

Pfizer beat Q1 but did not raise FY guidance.
Non-COVID growth and launched / acquired products confirm stabilization.
The Vyndamax settlement is the real structural positive for the post-2028 bridge.
Stabilization: medium-term visibility improved, near-term sentiment remains neutral.
PM focus: launched products, LOE bridge, Vyndamax, pipeline execution and dividend support.

PM read: this is a stabilization / balance-sheet improvement print. It is not a top-line acceleration print. The May 6 call and CEO transition commentary are the forward gates.

<<DIS_EARNINGS_BEGIN>>
TICKER: DIS
COMPANY: The Walt Disney Company
SECTOR: Communication Services
SUBSECTOR: Entertainment / Streaming / Parks / Sports
COUNTRY: United States
PUBLICATION_DATE: 2026-05-06
EVENT: Q2 FY2026
STATE_TRANSITION: Improvement
IMPORTANCE: ****
MARKET_REACTION: Positive
MARKET_REACTION_DETAIL: Shares rose roughly 7%-8.6% as investors rewarded the streaming profitability inflection, parks resilience, raised EPS guide and larger buyback plan.
PM_READ: Disney delivered a genuine thesis-improvement print as streaming crossed double-digit margin, parks held up despite traffic pressure, and management raised FY EPS growth and buybacks.
THEMES: Streaming profitability, Disney+, parks resilience, consumer K-shape, fuel risk, ESPN, NFL Network, buybacks, D'Amaro strategy reset, IP monetization
SEGMENT_TABLE_STATUS: Full
SOURCE_ROLE: Full note

EXEC SUMMARY

Improvement. Disney delivered revenue of $25.17B, up 7% YoY and ahead of consensus, with adjusted EPS of $1.57, up 8% YoY and above the Street. The most important signal was not the headline beat but the SVOD profitability inflection: Entertainment streaming revenue rose 13% and SVOD operating income rose 88%, producing a 10.6% margin, the first double-digit streaming margin quarter.

The parks / experiences signal was resilient but more nuanced. Experiences revenue reached a fiscal Q2 record, but domestic park attendance fell 1% while domestic per-capita spending rose 5%, showing a clear K-shaped consumer mix. CFO commentary on fuel risk matters because the Middle East energy shock can eventually affect lower-frequency park visitors.

PM read: the streaming bear case is materially weakened, D'Amaro’s first call gave investors a cleaner strategic frame, and the FY guide / buyback raise confirm management confidence. The remaining watch item is whether parks traffic holds if fuel and travel costs rise further.

OFFICIAL EARNINGS DETAIL

Revenue / Sales:
Revenue was $25.17B, up 7% YoY and above consensus of roughly $24.85B-$25.03B.

Consensus:
Adjusted EPS consensus was roughly $1.49-$1.50, and revenue consensus was roughly $24.85B-$25.03B. Disney beat both.

EPS / Earnings:
Adjusted EPS was $1.57, up 8% YoY. GAAP EPS was $1.27, down 30% YoY from $1.81, reflecting a higher tax bill and ESPN / NFL Network acquisition-related costs.

Profitability / Margins:
Total segment operating income was $4.6B, up 4% YoY and above prior guidance. SVOD operating income was $582M, up 88% YoY, with a 10.6% margin.

Key KPI:
The key KPI is SVOD margin crossing 10% one quarter ahead of the full-year target, combined with Entertainment streaming revenue acceleration from +11% in Q1 to +13% in Q2.

Guidance headline:
FY2026 adjusted EPS growth guidance was raised to roughly 12% excluding the 53rd week and roughly 16% including it. Q3 total segment operating income was guided around $5.3B, and FY2027 double-digit adjusted EPS growth was targeted.

Capital allocation:
Share repurchase target increased to at least $8B from at least $7B. Six-month FCF declined to $2.66B from $5.63B due to higher income tax payments and increased content / parks investment.

ACCOUNTING / EPS QUALITY CHECK

Reported earnings quality:
Mixed

Adjustment issue:
GAAP EPS was depressed by a higher tax bill and ESPN NFL Network / NFL RedZone / NFL Fantasy acquisition costs, with the ESPN NFL-related transaction diluting FY adjusted EPS by roughly $0.03.

PM interpretation:
Use adjusted EPS, segment operating income, SVOD margin, streaming revenue growth and parks per-capita / attendance mix as the cleaner measures. GAAP EPS is less useful this quarter because it reflects tax and transaction-cost effects rather than the operating inflection.

SEGMENT / BUSINESS BREAKDOWN

SEGMENT TABLE

| Segment | % of Total | Revenue | YoY Growth | Margin Trend | Key Commentary |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| Entertainment | ~47% | $11.72B | +10% | Improving | Streaming profitability inflection drove market reaction. |
| Experiences | ~38% | $9.5B | +7% | Mixed | Fiscal Q2 record, but domestic attendance -1%. |
| Sports | ~12% | $2.9B | +2% | Stable | ESPN integration and DTC app matter for future structure. |
| SVOD / Entertainment streaming | Embedded | $5.49B | +13% | Margin 10.6% | First double-digit margin quarter, +88% operating income. |
| Domestic parks | Embedded | Not separately disclosed | Attendance -1%; spend +5% | K-shaped | Traffic weaker, spend per guest stronger. |
| Cruise / international parks | Embedded | Not confirmed | Strong cruise demand | Positive | World of Frozen / Adventure pre-opening costs suppress flow-through. |

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

Disney raised FY2026 adjusted EPS growth guidance and lifted the buyback target to at least $8B. Management also targeted double-digit adjusted EPS growth in FY2027, giving the market a two-year earnings bridge rather than a one-quarter beat.

Revision direction: upward revisions. Analysts should raise streaming margin confidence, FY2026 EPS assumptions and buyback assumptions. Parks traffic assumptions may remain cautious because domestic attendance fell and CFO commentary flagged fuel risk.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

The market cared about streaming profitability more than reported GAAP EPS. For three years, the core Disney bear case was that streaming could scale subscribers but not structural profit. Q2 directly challenges that: SVOD operating income rose 88% and margin reached 10.6%, one quarter ahead of target.

The second focus was D'Amaro’s first earnings call as CEO. The “Disney+ as digital centerpiece” framing matters because it turns Disney+ from a standalone streaming service into the cross-platform hub for sports, games, parks and IP monetization. That gives investors a cleaner architecture for the post-Iger strategy.

The reaction was rational. The print delivered on revenue, adjusted EPS, streaming margin, parks record revenue, FY guide and buybacks at the same time. The only real cautions were GAAP EPS distortion, weaker six-month FCF and domestic parks attendance.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality signal is SVOD margin at 10.6%. It converts streaming from a debated investment sink into a visible profit pool. The second-quality signal is the FY2026 / FY2027 EPS bridge, because management is now underwriting multi-year growth rather than hiding behind transition language.

The most fragile signal is the parks mix. Domestic attendance down 1% with per-capita spend up 5% is good for revenue today but not a clean broad-consumer signal. If fuel prices rise further, lower-income / lower-frequency visitors may pull back more visibly.

Analysts are likely to raise streaming margin assumptions and FY EPS estimates, but remain careful on parks attendance and FCF. Consensus is probably too low on streaming profitability and broadly right to cautious on parks traffic.

State transition: Improvement

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether Disney could make streaming structurally profitable without damaging parks and core IP investment. Q2 says the streaming profit inflection is now real. The debate now shifts from “can Disney+ make money?” to “how much margin can Disney sustain while investing in sports, parks, games and AI?”

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

Print reaction function:
The market was pricing a company still proving streaming economics under a new CEO. The print changed that debate because SVOD moved above 10% margin, the FY guide was raised, and buybacks were increased. The +7% to +8.6% move makes sense because the highest-duration part of the thesis, streaming, moved from promise to profit. What needs to happen next is sustained SVOD margin and no material parks traffic deterioration. What would break the thesis is a fuel-driven attendance decline combined with higher content / parks capex that prevents FCF recovery.

Stock identity:
Quality compounder, turnaround, expensive but deliverable. The print moves Disney away from “legacy media value trap” and closer to a re-accelerating IP compounder with a streaming profit engine. It is not yet a clean beat-and-raise machine because parks traffic and FCF remain watch items, but the identity improved materially.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

Shares rose sharply as investors rewarded the streaming profitability milestone and raised guidance. Read-across is positive for streaming platforms that can show pricing power and margin, and positive for IP owners with multi-channel monetization. The parks read-across is mixed: high-end spend is resilient, but domestic attendance softness hints at consumer bifurcation.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

SVOD margin / Why it matters: The re-rating depends on streaming staying structurally profitable.
Domestic parks attendance / Why it matters: A -1% attendance print is the clearest consumer caution in the quarter.
Fuel prices / Why it matters: CFO flagged fuel as a possible future consumer behavior risk.
FCF recovery / Why it matters: Buybacks and valuation need cash conversion to normalize.
Disney+ hub strategy / Why it matters: D'Amaro’s strategic reset depends on Disney+ becoming the cross-platform engagement layer.

BOTTOM LINE

Disney delivered a genuine improvement print.
Streaming crossed the profitability threshold the market had been waiting for.
Parks revenue is resilient but consumer mix is K-shaped.
FY EPS guide and buybacks moved higher.
The stock reaction is justified; next test is sustaining SVOD margin while parks traffic holds.

<<DIS_EARNINGS_END>>

<<UBER_EARNINGS_BEGIN>>
TICKER: UBER
COMPANY: Uber Technologies, Inc.
SECTOR: Industrials / Consumer Internet
SUBSECTOR: Mobility / Delivery / Platform Marketplace / Autonomous Vehicle Partnerships
COUNTRY: United States
PUBLICATION_DATE: 2026-05-06
EVENT: Q1 2026
STATE_TRANSITION: Improvement
IMPORTANCE: ****
MARKET_REACTION: Positive
MARKET_REACTION_DETAIL: Shares rose roughly 8%-10% as investors looked through the revenue miss and rewarded bookings growth, Q2 EBITDA guidance and consumer resilience commentary.
PM_READ: Uber’s Q1 confirms a stronger platform-quality story: bookings, trips, EBITDA and FCF are compounding, while the reported revenue miss is accounting noise from business-model changes.
THEMES: Mobility, delivery, Uber One, bookings, consumer resilience, fuel shock, EBITDA guide, FCF, buybacks, Waymo, autonomous vehicles
SEGMENT_TABLE_STATUS: Partial
SOURCE_ROLE: Full note

EXEC SUMMARY

Improvement. Uber reported gross bookings of $53.7B, up 25% YoY and ahead of consensus, with trips up 20%, MAPCs up 17% to 199M, adjusted EBITDA up 33% to $2.481B, and FCF of $2.3B. Revenue of $13.2B missed consensus by about $90M, but that was driven by business-model changes rather than demand weakness.

The Q2 guide was the operative catalyst. Gross bookings guidance of $56.25B-$57.75B was above consensus, and adjusted EBITDA guidance of $2.70B-$2.80B was materially above consensus around $2.47B. That forward EBITDA guide drove the stock reaction.

PM read: Uber is improving from growth platform to cash-generative compounder. The macro read-across is also important: management saw little consumer impact from the fuel shock, while Uber One reached 50M members and now drives roughly half of Mobility / Delivery bookings.

OFFICIAL EARNINGS DETAIL

Revenue / Sales:
Revenue was $13.2B, up 14% YoY but below consensus around $13.29B. The miss reflected business-model changes that reduced reported revenue growth by roughly 9 percentage points, not demand weakness.

Consensus:
Revenue consensus was roughly $13.29B. Non-GAAP EPS consensus was around $0.70-$0.71. Q2 gross bookings consensus was around $56.17B and Q2 adjusted EBITDA consensus around $2.47B.

EPS / Earnings:
Non-GAAP EPS was $0.72, up 44% YoY and above consensus. GAAP EPS was $0.13, below the expected $0.70 due to a $1.5B pre-tax equity investment mark-to-market loss on holdings such as Didi and Grab.

Profitability / Margins:
Adjusted EBITDA was $2.481B, up 33% YoY, equal to 4.6% of gross bookings, up 20bps. GAAP income from operations was $1.9B, up 57% YoY. FCF was $2.3B.

Key KPI:
Gross bookings +25%, trips +20%, MAPCs +17% and Uber One at 50M members.

Guidance headline:
Q2 gross bookings guided to $56.25B-$57.75B. Q2 adjusted EBITDA guided to $2.70B-$2.80B, materially above consensus.

Capital allocation:
Uber repurchased $3B of stock in Q1. Buybacks are now a meaningful part of the equity story.

ACCOUNTING / EPS QUALITY CHECK

Reported earnings quality:
Distorted on GAAP; clean on non-GAAP / operating metrics

Adjustment issue:
GAAP EPS was distorted by a $1.5B pre-tax equity investment mark-to-market loss tied to equity holdings including Didi and Grab.

PM interpretation:
Use gross bookings, trips, adjusted EBITDA, FCF, non-GAAP EPS and Uber One membership as the clean measures. GAAP EPS is not the operative performance metric this quarter.

SEGMENT / BUSINESS BREAKDOWN

SEGMENT TABLE

| Autonomous / Waymo | Strategic optionality | Not meaningful | Pilot expansion | Not confirmed | Austin and Atlanta confirmed; third city remains catalyst. |

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

Uber’s Q2 guide was strong enough to neutralize the revenue miss. Management guided Q2 adjusted EBITDA around $2.75B midpoint, materially above consensus, and bookings also above Street expectations.

Revision direction: upward revisions. Analysts should raise Q2 / FY EBITDA and FCF assumptions, while keeping reported revenue models adjusted for business-model accounting changes.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

The market correctly ignored the revenue miss because the miss was structural accounting, not demand. Gross bookings, trips, EBITDA and FCF are better measures of platform health. All were strong.

The consumer read-across is important. Uber has 199M MAPCs and management saw little impact from higher fuel prices on consumer demand. In a week dominated by Middle East energy shock concerns, Uber’s platform data argues against immediate consumer demand collapse.

The Waymo / robotaxi narrative remains unresolved. Austin and Atlanta are confirmed, but investors still want a third city and unit economics disclosure.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality signal is Q2 EBITDA guidance. An 11%+ guide beat on EBITDA shows operating leverage is compounding faster than the model. The second-quality signal is Uber One at 50M members and roughly half of Mobility / Delivery bookings.

The lowest-quality signal is GAAP EPS, because the equity mark-to-market loss makes the GAAP line unsuitable. The revenue miss is also low-quality as an operating signal because it was driven by business-model changes.

Analysts should raise EBITDA and FCF estimates, maintain a bookings-led demand model, and avoid overreacting to reported revenue.

State transition: Improvement

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether Uber could keep compounding bookings while expanding margins. Q1 answers yes. The debate now shifts to how much of the profitability acceleration is structural and when autonomous partnerships become visible in unit economics.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

Print reaction function:
The market was pricing a high-quality platform but still watching for consumer sensitivity to fuel and macro. Uber delivered bookings growth, EBITDA expansion, FCF, buybacks and consumer resilience commentary. The +8%-10% move makes sense because the Q2 EBITDA guide shifted the forward earnings slope higher. What needs to happen next is continued Uber One growth, stable frequency and AV partnership disclosure. What would break the thesis is consumer trip deceleration combined with higher driver incentives and weaker delivery margins.

Stock identity:
Quality compounder, GARP, beat-and-raise machine emerging. The print reinforces Uber’s transition from growth platform to durable cash compounder. The AV layer remains optionality rather than core identity today.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

Positive read-across for platform marketplaces, delivery, mobility and consumer services. The strongest peer read-across is to DASH because both Uber and DoorDash showed consumer resilience and strong forward demand despite fuel pressure.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

Uber One / Why it matters: Membership is the structural retention and frequency moat.
Q2 EBITDA conversion / Why it matters: The guide beat must translate into actual margin delivery.
Fuel sensitivity / Why it matters: Higher gas could still affect riders or drivers if sustained.
Waymo third city / Why it matters: AV optionality needs scale and unit economics.
Reported revenue accounting / Why it matters: Business-model changes can obscure underlying demand.

BOTTOM LINE

Uber’s revenue miss is not the story.
Bookings, trips, EBITDA, FCF and Q2 guide are the story.
Consumer demand appears resilient despite fuel pressure.
Improvement: Uber is becoming a cash-generative platform compounder.
Next catalyst: sustained EBITDA conversion and clearer AV economics.

Continuation. Arm delivered record Q4 FY2026 revenue of $1.49B, up 20% YoY, above consensus and above the midpoint of guidance. Q4 licensing revenue was a record $819M, up 29%, and Q4 royalty revenue was a record Q4 figure of $671M, up 11%.

The structural bull signal is data center royalties and AGI CPU demand. Data center royalties more than doubled YoY, and management expects them to double again in FY2027. AGI CPU demand moved from an initial $1B target to $2B, but supply constraints mean Arm cannot fully monetize that demand immediately.

PM read: the fundamental AI royalty thesis strengthened, but the stock had rallied 84% YTD and was priced for a flawless print. The AH reversal is a valuation / setup event, not a deterioration in Arm’s IP position.

Profitability / Margins:
Q4 non-GAAP operating income was $731M and non-GAAP operating margin was 49%. FY non-GAAP FCF was $882M.

Key KPI:
Data center royalties more than doubled YoY and are expected by management to double again in FY2027. AGI CPU demand increased from $1B to $2B.

Guidance headline:
Q1 FY2027 revenue guidance was $1.26B ±$50M, a sequential step-down from Q4 due to licensing lumpiness and supply-gated AGI CPU delivery. Non-GAAP EPS guidance was $0.31-$0.35.

Capital allocation:
Capital allocation not central to the quarter. The key value driver is royalty / licensing growth.

Reported earnings quality:
Clean

No material non-operating item identified in the supplied scout.

Use licensing revenue, royalty revenue, data center royalty growth, ACV, non-GAAP operating margin and AGI CPU demand as the clean performance metrics.

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

The Q1 FY2027 guide created the market tension: $1.26B midpoint implies a sequential step-down from Q4, even as demand for AGI CPU is accelerating. Management framed this around licensing lumpiness and supply limitations, not demand deterioration.

Revision direction: mix-shift revisions. FY AI / data-center royalty confidence should rise, but near-term Q1 revenue / EPS estimates may be constrained by supply and licensing-timing effects.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

The professional tape split between fundamentals and setup. Fundamentally, Arm delivered another record year, record licensing, record royalties and a very strong data-center royalty outlook. Setup-wise, the stock was up 84% YTD, options implied a large move, and investors needed more than confirmation.

The AGI CPU data point is unusual: demand doubled to $2B, but supply prevents full near-term monetization. That is bullish for duration and bearish for immediate quarterly revenue recognition.

The AH reversal was rational in market terms even though the operating print was strong. Arm did not miss the thesis; it failed to exceed a very stretched valuation setup.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality signal is data center royalties doubling, with management expecting another doubling in FY2027. This makes Arm a cleaner AI infrastructure royalty play than most hardware suppliers.

The most fragile signal is the Q1 FY2027 sequential revenue step-down, because it reminds investors that licensing revenue is lumpy and AGI CPU monetization is supply constrained. Smartphone softness also matters because mobile remains a large royalty base.

Analysts should raise confidence in medium-term data-center royalties and AGI CPU demand, but near-term quarterly models may be more conservative because the supply constraint delays revenue capture.

State transition: Continuation

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether Arm could extend its mobile royalty model into data center and AI. Q4 confirms that transition is working. The debate now shifts to timing: demand is visible, but supply and licensing lumpiness determine when it appears in revenue.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

Print reaction function:
The market was pricing near-perfection after an 84% YTD rally. Arm delivered record results and strong AI demand, but the Q1 sequential step-down and supply-gated AGI CPU ramp were enough to trigger profit taking. The move is a setup reset, not a thesis break. What needs to happen next is supply conversion into revenue, continued data-center royalty doubling and better visibility on AGI CPU shipments. What would break the thesis is if royalty growth slows before AGI CPU monetization ramps.

Stock identity:
High-growth compounder, expensive but deliverable, setup-risk stock. The print reinforces Arm’s high-quality AI royalty identity, but the valuation reaction shows it is also a setup-risk stock when expectations run ahead of monetization timing.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

The reaction is mixed: positive for the AI IP thesis, negative for near-term valuation absorption. Read-across is positive for semiconductor IP and data-center CPU demand, but cautionary for high-multiple AI infrastructure stocks where supply constraints delay revenue recognition.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

Data center royalties / Why it matters: This is the cleanest AI monetization metric.
AGI CPU supply / Why it matters: Demand doubled, but revenue is supply-gated.
Q1 FY2027 revenue / Why it matters: Sequential step-down created the stock reversal.
Smartphone softness / Why it matters: Mobile remains an important royalty base.
Valuation setup / Why it matters: Strong fundamentals may not be enough after large YTD gains.

BOTTOM LINE

Arm’s fundamentals remain strong.
AI royalty thesis is intact and data-center royalties are doubling.
AGI CPU demand doubled but cannot be fully monetized immediately.
The AH reversal is valuation / setup, not fundamental deterioration.
Continuation: durable AI IP winner, but priced for execution.

Clean

Revision direction: upward revisions to GOV / demand assumptions; limited or mixed near-term EBITDA revisions because management is investing in gas relief and replatforming; more constructive H2 margin revisions if investment spend normalizes.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

The market cared about the guide, not the revenue miss. The Q1 revenue shortfall was backward-looking and explainable through weather, integration and mix. GOV and guidance are better forward measures of the business.

The consumer read-across is strong. DoorDash showed record MAUs, record membership sign-ups and robust GOV growth, aligning with Uber’s message that demand remains resilient despite fuel pressure.

The AI productivity disclosure matters. If more than half, and close to two-thirds, of code is now written with AI assistance, product velocity and replatforming speed may improve structurally.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality signal is Q2 GOV guidance. It shows that management sees better demand into Q2 than the market had modeled. The second signal is membership / MAU records, which support frequency and retention.

The lowest-quality signal is the revenue miss and order miss versus consensus. These are not thesis-breaking because GOV, EPS, EBITDA and guidance were stronger, but they are worth monitoring if repeated.

Analysts should raise GOV and demand assumptions, keep near-term margin estimates cautious due to gas relief and replatforming, and watch whether H2 margin recovery materializes.

State transition: Improvement

What needs to happen next is H2 margin recovery after gas relief and replatforming spend. What would break the thesis is if GOV strength does not convert into EBITDA margin improvement.

High-growth compounder, GARP, expensive but deliverable, setup-risk stock. The print reinforces the growth-compounder identity, but valuation remains sensitive to any margin disappointment.

Read-across is also positive for AI-enabled software productivity narratives inside consumer platforms.

Profitability / Margins:
Adjusted EBITDA was $1.56B, up 66% YoY, at a record 85% margin, up roughly 400bps YoY. FCF was $1.29B, elevated by tax timing, with FY2026 normalized conversion expected around 75% of EBITDA.

Key KPI:
Consumer vertical advertiser spend accelerated sharply: March +25% versus January, and April reached an all-time monthly high, above prior Q4 peaks.

Guidance headline:
Q2 revenue was guided to $1.915B-$1.945B, implying roughly +52% to +55% YoY and +4% to +6% QoQ. Q2 adjusted EBITDA was guided to $1.615B-$1.645B, implying 84%-85% margin.

AppLovin repurchased $1B of stock in Q1, equal to 2.23M shares, and had $2.3B remaining under authorization.

Clean

No material non-operating income item identified. FCF was elevated by tax timing, so run-rate FCF should be normalized around management’s FY conversion framework.

The market cared less about the headline beat than about the forward architecture. AppLovin is already growing revenue at +59% with an 85% EBITDA margin; the incremental question is whether consumer vertical and public Axon access create another multi-quarter growth runway.

Management tone also mattered. Foroughi avoided defending the stock or addressing short-seller noise and instead framed the company as entering its best forward setup. That tone shift matters because prior debates often centered on sustainability and credibility.

The delayed stock reaction is also relevant. The initial move was not explosive, but the stock strengthened as investors processed the call detail on consumer vertical acceleration and the June public launch. That suggests more fundamental absorption than pure headline momentum.

Analysts are likely to raise revenue, EBITDA and TAM confidence assumptions, but may keep some valuation discount for execution risk around self-serve launch and regulatory / short-seller overhang.

EXEC SUMMARY

Stabilization. WBD reported revenue of $8.89B, essentially flat YoY and in line with consensus, but GAAP EPS of -$1.17 was massively distorted by a $2.8B Netflix termination fee and $1.3B of acquisition-related amortization / restructuring charges. The cleaner operating metric was adjusted EBITDA of $2.20B, ahead of consensus by roughly 12%.

The business is not collapsing: streaming subscribers exceeded 140M globally, up 14% YoY, and management expects to finish 2026 above 150M. Studios also have a stronger creative slate, including 11 Academy Awards and the future Harry Potter series. But FCF was negative $476M, advertising declined and leverage remains the structural constraint.

PM read: the market correctly ignored the GAAP EPS catastrophe, but it also refused to reward the EBITDA beat because WBD still has not shown the cash-flow bridge needed for deleveraging and streaming transition credibility.

Profitability / Margins:
Adjusted EBITDA was $2.20B, beating consensus by roughly 11.8%. Operating margin was -27.8% on a GAAP basis because of transaction-related costs. FCF was -$476M versus +$302M prior year.

Key KPI:
Global streaming subscribers exceeded 140M, up 14% YoY, with management guiding to more than 150M by year-end 2026.

FY2026 subscriber target above 150M. WB Studios targets at least $3B of annual adjusted EBITDA from an 18-film slate in 2027.

Capital allocation remains constrained by high leverage and negative FCF. Deleveraging remains the central investor requirement.

Distorted

Adjustment issue:
GAAP EPS was distorted by the $2.8B Netflix termination fee linked to the pending Paramount Skydance merger, plus $1.3B of pre-tax acquisition-related amortization and restructuring charges.

PM interpretation:
Use adjusted EBITDA, FCF, streaming subscriber growth, advertising revenue, and leverage metrics as the cleaner measures. GAAP EPS is not the operative measure this quarter.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

The market treated the GAAP EPS loss as noise because it was transaction-driven. The stock did not collapse despite the enormous reported EPS miss, which shows investors understood the accounting distortion.

But WBD also did not rally on the EBITDA beat because the key institutional issue is FCF and leverage. Adjusted EBITDA can beat, but if FCF is negative, deleveraging remains delayed and the equity multiple stays capped.

The reduced streaming disclosure is a governance / transparency issue. At the moment WBD wants investors to believe in streaming scale, it is giving them less granular subscriber / ARPU detail.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality signal is adjusted EBITDA beating consensus by almost 12% while streaming subscribers grew to more than 140M. That shows the operating base is stabilizing.

The lowest-quality signal is GAAP EPS, which is distorted, but the most important negative signal is FCF deterioration. Negative FCF is the real constraint for a highly levered media company.

Analysts may raise EBITDA estimates modestly but remain cautious on FCF, leverage, merger risk and streaming-disclosure quality. Consensus is unlikely to re-rate the stock until cash flow improves.

State transition: Stabilization

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether WBD could transition from linear decline to streaming scale while reducing leverage. Q1 shows operational stabilization but not enough cash conversion. The debate remains whether streaming and studios can offset linear decay quickly enough to fix the balance sheet.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

Print reaction function:
The market was pricing WBD as a high-leverage transition story with poor earnings visibility. The print did not worsen the thesis because the GAAP EPS miss was transaction noise and adjusted EBITDA beat. But it also did not fix the thesis because FCF stayed negative. What needs to happen next is sustained EBITDA, positive FCF, clearer merger path and deleveraging. What would break the thesis is continued FCF burn combined with regulatory delays or blocked merger economics.

Stock identity:
Turnaround, value trap risk, optionality stock. The print supports stabilization but not a full turnaround. WBD remains a high-leverage optionality name where the equity can work only if streaming scale, studio recovery and deleveraging align.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

The flat stock reaction was rational. The market looked through the GAAP loss but did not capitalize the EBITDA beat because cash flow and leverage remain unresolved. Read-across to media is mixed: streaming scale is possible, but legacy linear and transaction complexity continue to suppress equity value.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

Streaming disclosure / Why it matters: Reduced subscriber / ARPU detail may lower investor confidence.
Merger regulatory review / Why it matters: FCC scrutiny adds timing and deal-completion risk.
Advertising ex-NBA / Why it matters: NBA absence created a major ad headwind.

Consensus:

Profitability / Margins:

Billings rose 31% YoY, Secure Networking billings rose 32%, OT billings rose 70%, and AI-driven security operations billings rose 23%.

Guidance headline:
FY2026 billings guidance was raised to $8.8B-$9.1B. FY revenue guidance was $7.71B-$7.87B. FY non-GAAP EPS guidance was $3.10-$3.16, above consensus around $2.91. Q2 EPS guidance of $0.72-$0.76 was also above consensus around $0.68.

Capital allocation:
FCF generation is the main capital-allocation signal. The scout also notes roughly $10B returned to shareholders via buybacks since the 2009 IPO.

Clean

No material non-operating item identified.

The market cared about the magnitude of the EPS beat and the billings acceleration. A 32%-34% EPS beat combined with +31% billings and a raised FY EPS guide is enough to force a real model reset.

The most important sector read-across was JPMorgan’s question on Check Point divergence. Fortinet growing Secure Networking billings +32% while a peer decelerates implies share gain and platform displacement, not simply an improving market.

AI is now part of the cybersecurity demand function. Management explicitly linked AI to more complex threats, OT billings rose 70%, and FortiGate G-series targets AI data-center security workloads.

The lowest-quality or most fragile signal is valuation absorption. RBC staying Sector Perform despite the print shows some investors may see the business as high quality but not cheap.

Analysts will likely raise billings, EPS, FCF and product revenue estimates. Consensus was too low on operating leverage and product-cycle strength.

What needs to happen next is sustained billings growth, continued product revenue strength, and proof that OT / AI security remain durable growth vectors. What would break the thesis is if Q2 billings normalize sharply or product growth proves one-off.

Stock identity:
Quality compounder, GARP, beat-and-raise machine. Fortinet’s Q1 reinforces a high-quality platform identity: strong billings, high margins, record FCF and visible AI / OT growth. It is not a speculative AI name; it is a cash-generative cybersecurity compounder with AI-driven demand acceleration.

The positive AH move was justified by the EPS beat, billings acceleration and guidance raise. Read-across is negative for slower firewall peers and positive for cybersecurity platforms exposed to AI threat complexity, OT security and secure networking convergence.

Valuation / Why it matters: Strong fundamentals may still face multiple discipline.

Billings +31%, product +41% and EPS +32%-34% beat consensus.
Check Point divergence makes this a share-gain story.
AI and OT security are now real growth accelerants.
FY guidance raise should drive upward revisions.

Improvement. Zillow reported official revenue of $708M, up 18.4% YoY and slightly above consensus, with adjusted EPS of $0.53, adjusted EBITDA of $182M and FCF of $127M. The operating quarter was broadly strong: mortgage revenue rose 56%, purchase loan origination volume rose 96%, rentals revenue rose 30%, and enhanced markets reached 49% of total connections.

The stock reaction was negative because the market looked forward, not backward. Management said the housing market is effectively flat and is not planning for improvement. Q2 EBITDA guidance was below expectations, and the FTC trial remains a structural risk.

PM read: the company is executing a platform transition in a frozen housing market. Zillow is gaining share in mortgage and rentals, but the equity will struggle to re-rate until EBITDA guidance clears and housing turnover stops acting as a ceiling.

Profitability / Margins:

Capital allocation not central in the supplied scout. The key financial issue is EBITDA conversion and FCF growth.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

The professional tape focused on the disconnect between Q1 operating quality and Q2 guide quality. Zillow beat on adjusted EPS and EBITDA and showed acceleration across mortgage, rentals and enhanced markets, but the call emphasized a flat housing market and softer Q2 EBITDA.

The official revenue discrepancy also matters for investor communication. The $708M official revenue figure appears to be the correct comparison metric, while the $748M figure in other sources creates headline confusion.

The stock reaction was rational in the short term because investors were not arguing with Q1; they were discounting Q2 EBITDA and the housing backdrop.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality signal is mortgage: $1.5B of purchase loan origination volume, up 96%, and Zillow Home Loans now a top-25 national lender. That supports the thesis that Zillow is becoming a transaction platform rather than just a listings marketplace.

The second-quality signal is rentals +30%, which is valuable because it is less dependent on housing turnover. Enhanced markets at 49% of connections shows the integrated model is progressing.

The weakest signal is Q2 EBITDA guidance. The company can be structurally improving but still disappoint the stock if growth investments and flat housing keep near-term margins below expectations.

The prior debate was whether Zillow could grow in a frozen housing market. Q1 says it can, through mortgage, rentals and enhanced markets. The debate now shifts to whether those engines can produce margin expansion quickly enough to satisfy investors before housing turnover recovers.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

Print reaction function:
The market initially bought the Q1 beat, then sold the call detail. That makes sense: the backward-looking operating data were strong, but the Q2 EBITDA guide and flat housing assumption lowered near-term earnings confidence. What needs to happen next is Q2 EBITDA delivery, continued mortgage share gain and enhanced-market conversion toward the 75% target. What would break the thesis is a housing slowdown that hurts residential while investment spending prevents EBITDA leverage.

Stock identity:
Turnaround, GARP, setup-risk stock. Zillow is improving operationally but remains highly sensitive to housing macro and guide quality. The platform transition is real, but the stock is not yet a beat-and-raise machine.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

The reaction was mixed-to-negative. Initial AH buying reflected the Q1 beat; later selling reflected Q2 EBITDA guidance and the frozen housing comment. Read-across is positive for proptech platforms gaining share in mortgage and rentals, but negative for any housing-exposed equity dependent on a near-term transaction recovery.

Rentals growth / Why it matters: +30% growth is insulated from housing turnover.
Enhanced markets penetration / Why it matters: 49% of connections must move toward the 75% target.

Zillow’s Q1 operations improved materially.
Mortgage, rentals and enhanced markets are all moving in the right direction.

<<MCD_EARNINGS_BEGIN>>
TICKER: MCD
COMPANY: McDonald's Corporation
SECTOR: Consumer Discretionary / Consumer Staples
SUBSECTOR: Quick Service Restaurants / Global Franchised Foodservice
COUNTRY: United States
PUBLICATION_DATE: 2026-05-07
EVENT: Q1 2026
STATE_TRANSITION: Stabilization
IMPORTANCE: ****
MARKET_REACTION: Mixed
MARKET_REACTION_DETAIL: Initial earnings-beat enthusiasm faded as investors focused on U.S. company-operated margin pressure, Q2 deceleration risk and lower-income consumer pressure.
PM_READ: McDonald’s delivered a clean headline beat and positive global comps, but the print was FX-aided and the call shifted attention back to consumer pressure, U.S. owned-store margins and possible refranchising.
THEMES: Value menu, low-income consumer, QSR traffic, FX-aided EPS, company-operated margins, refranchising review, Q2 deceleration, fuel and grocery pressure, World Cup, beverage platform
SEGMENT_TABLE_STATUS: Full
SOURCE_ROLE: Full note

EXEC SUMMARY

Stabilization. McDonald’s Q1 was numerically better than feared: global comparable sales rose 3.8%, U.S. comps rose 3.9%, revenue grew 9% reported and 4% constant currency, and adjusted EPS of $2.83 beat consensus while GAAP EPS was $2.78. The company also disclosed $47 million of restructuring charges and noted that adjusted EPS growth was only 1% in constant currency, which keeps the quarter from reading as a clean acceleration.

The market issue was not the Q1 print itself; it was the quality of the beat and the forward setup. The stock fade makes sense because the call pushed investors toward U.S. company-operated margin pressure, elevated fuel / grocery pressure on lower-income consumers, and a likely Q2 comp deceleration against a difficult prior-year promotional compare.

The under-discussed signal is strategic rather than quarterly: management is openly evaluating whether some U.S. company-operated restaurants are best held by McDonald’s or moved into stronger operator hands. That is not an earnings crisis, but it is a P&L mix and capital-allocation signal that matters beyond Q1.

| Guidance / Outlook | FY26 targets reaffirmed; FX EPS tailwind expected at $0.20-$0.30 | Prior guidance reaffirmed | In line | Not applicable | Not applicable | Reaffirmed guide was offset by explicit Q2 deceleration risk and consumer-pressure commentary. |
| Market reaction | Beat-and-fade; modestly negative net reaction | Not applicable | Mixed | Not applicable | Not applicable | Initial beat reaction was not sustained as investors refocused on margin, consumer and Q2 comparison risk. |

| KPI | Actual | Prior / Benchmark | Change | PM Comment |

OFFICIAL EARNINGS DETAIL

Revenue / Sales:
McDonald’s reported consolidated revenue growth of 9% and 4% in constant currencies, with global systemwide sales up 11% and 6% in constant currencies. Global comps were +3.8%, with U.S. +3.9%, International Operated Markets +3.9%, and International Developmental Licensed Markets +3.4%.

Consensus:
Consensus was mixed across sources. The scout range of $6.47B-$6.53B implies a small revenue beat or high-end match depending on the provider used; the safer archive treatment is a modest beat, not a blowout.

EPS / Earnings:
GAAP diluted EPS was $2.78, up 7% reported and 2% constant currency. Adjusted EPS excluding the $0.05 restructuring charge was $2.83, up 6% reported but only 1% constant currency, with the call transcript identifying a $0.13 FX benefit.

Profitability / Margins:
Consolidated operating income rose 12% reported and 6% constant currency; excluding restructuring charges, consolidated operating income rose 11% reported and 5% constant currency. The transcript summary also flags more than $3.6B of restaurant margins and a 46% adjusted operating margin, while management discussion focused investor concern on U.S. company-operated margins.

Key KPI:
The highest-quality operating KPI is global comparable sales, because all three geographic segments were positive. The lower-quality element is constant-currency EPS growth, because +1% adjusted constant-currency EPS growth does not match the stronger headline EPS beat.

Guidance headline:
Full-year 2026 targets were reaffirmed and FX was expected to contribute $0.20-$0.30 to EPS for the year, but the market focused more on Q2 deceleration risk and consumer-pressure commentary than on the reaffirmation.

Capital allocation:
Capital allocation was not the center of the Q1 debate. The relevant capital-allocation angle is the strategic review of company-operated ownership versus franchisee operation and the potential implications for mix, margin and invested capital.

ACCOUNTING / EPS QUALITY CHECK

Reported earnings quality:
Mixed.

Adjustment issue:
The main adjustments were a $47M pre-tax restructuring charge linked to Accelerating the Organization and a meaningful FX benefit to EPS; no major non-operating gain drove the beat.

PM interpretation:
Investors should focus less on headline adjusted EPS and more on constant-currency EPS growth, comparable sales quality, company-operated margins and whether value-driven traffic can be monetized without eroding unit economics.

SEGMENT / BUSINESS BREAKDOWN

SEGMENT TABLE

| Segment | % of Total | Revenue | YoY Growth | Margin Trend | Key Commentary |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| U.S. | Not disclosed | Not confirmed | Comparable sales +3.9% | Company-operated margin pressure | Value menu and check growth supported comps, but margin commentary was the core negative signal. |
| International Operated Markets | Not disclosed | Not confirmed | Comparable sales +3.9% | Not confirmed | U.K., Germany and Australia contributed to broad global performance. |
| International Developmental Licensed Markets | Not disclosed | Not confirmed | Comparable sales +3.4% | Not confirmed | Japan was highlighted in the scout as a key driver, keeping global comp strength diversified. |

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

McDonald’s reaffirmed FY26 targets and guided to a $0.20-$0.30 EPS tailwind from FX, but this does not create a clean upward-revision story because Q2 comparable sales are expected to slow against the prior-year Minecraft promotion and management acknowledged a pressured consumer backdrop. Revision direction: no material consensus change to mild mix-shift revisions, with models likely to preserve FY EPS while lowering confidence in U.S. owned-store margin and Q2 comp cadence.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

The professional tape should be read as beat-and-fade, not a failed quarter. McDonald’s cleared the near-term comp and EPS bar, but the market treated the print as a stress test of whether value menus can stabilize lower-income traffic without damaging restaurant economics. The answer was only partly reassuring.

The market’s concern is rational. A QSR stock can rally on value-led comps if traffic, franchisee profitability and margins all cooperate. Here, the comp line cooperated, but the call reintroduced two frictions: cost-sensitive consumers remain under pressure and company-operated restaurants are not delivering acceptable margin performance.

The sector read-across is that value is working, but not for free. That matters for QSR peers because value messaging can defend share while simultaneously compressing restaurant-level economics if labor, beef, fuel and promotion costs remain elevated.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The best signal is the breadth of comparable sales growth across all three geographic divisions. The weakest signal is the gap between reported EPS growth and constant-currency EPS growth, plus the direct management focus on U.S. company-operated margins.

Analysts are likely to keep the global comp trajectory intact but scrutinize U.S. margin bridge assumptions, Q2 deceleration, and whether the new beverage platform can create incremental traffic without requiring excessive promotion.

State transition: Stabilization

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether McDonald’s value reset could repair traffic and affordability perception without turning into a margin give-up. Q1 confirms the sales side of the value strategy, but not yet the margin side. The thesis is stabilized, not upgraded.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

Print reaction function:
The market was pricing a defensive consumer compounder that needed evidence of value-led traffic repair. The comp beat gave that evidence, but the move faded because the quality of EPS, Q2 deceleration risk and U.S. owned-store margin pressure prevented a clean rerating. For the stock to rerate, the next test is whether Q2 deceleration is only a promotional comparison issue rather than a renewed traffic problem. The thesis breaks if lower-income traffic weakens further while company-operated margins require more aggressive refranchising or value subsidization.

Stock identity:
defensive compounder, yield / income stock, quality compounder. The print reinforces the defensive compounder identity because global comps remained positive in a difficult consumer backdrop, but it challenges the quality-compounder premium because operating quality is not clean when FX, value spend and owned-store margin issues are central to the discussion.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

The stock reaction was mixed-to-negative because the market looked through the beat and focused on the call. The relevant peer read-across is negative for any QSR name relying on value to defend traffic while food, labor or fuel pressure remains elevated; it is more constructive for franchise-heavy models with less company-operated margin exposure.

For restaurants, the quarter says consumer demand is not collapsing, but the consumer is price-sensitive. That is a better read for traffic stabilization than for margin expansion.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

Q2 comparable sales / Why it matters: The Minecraft comparison and consumer pressure will determine whether Q1 was a temporary value-led stabilization or the start of a stronger comp path.

U.S. company-operated margins / Why it matters: Owned-store margin weakness could trigger refranchising or reveal that value-led traffic is not sufficiently profitable.

Lower-income traffic / Why it matters: McDonald’s is a direct read on budget-stretched consumers and affordability positioning.

Beverage platform / Why it matters: A successful McCafé beverage push could add higher-frequency traffic and improve mix.

Investor Day / Why it matters: Management may clarify ownership mix, refranchising posture and margin recovery path.

BOTTOM LINE

McDonald’s beat, but not cleanly enough to reset the debate.

The quarter stabilizes the sales thesis.

It does not remove the margin / consumer pressure overlay.

PM focus should stay on Q2 comps, U.S. owned-store margin repair and whether refranchising becomes a real strategic lever.

MARKET_REACTION_DETAIL: Revenue beat and FY guide raise were offset by EPS miss optics, premium valuation and short-term profit-taking after recent upgrades.
PM_READ: Airbnb’s operating trajectory improved through revenue acceleration, GBV growth, app mix, first-time bookers and a guide raise, but EPS optics were noisy and the market still needs proof that new categories and international expansion can sustain growth.
THEMES: Travel demand, GBV, nights and seats booked, ADR, app booking mix, Reserve Now Pay Later, Middle East cancellations, World Cup, experiences, services, AI support, fee simplification, buybacks
SEGMENT_TABLE_STATUS: Partial
SOURCE_ROLE: Full note

EXEC SUMMARY

Improvement. Airbnb delivered a strong operating quarter: revenue grew 18% to $2.7B, GBV rose 19% to $29.2B, nights and seats booked grew 9%, net income was $160M, adjusted EBITDA was $519M and free cash flow was $1.7B. Management also raised full-year revenue guidance to low-to-mid-teens growth and guided Q2 revenue to $3.54B-$3.60B.

The scout’s operating read is right, but the accounting bridge needs nuance. Airbnb disclosed both a roughly $70M one-time gain from the sale of a privately held equity investment and a roughly $70M deferred-tax-asset adjustment from U.S. Corporate Alternative Minimum Tax changes; these offset directionally, so the EPS miss should not be treated as a simple one-way tax add-back.

The market reaction was therefore mixed rather than fundamentally bearish. Revenue, GBV, app penetration, first-time bookers, expansion markets and guidance all moved in the right direction, but the stock did not get a clean rerating because premium-valuation travel names need both operating acceleration and clean EPS / FCF optics.

OFFICIAL EARNINGS DETAIL

Revenue / Sales:
Revenue was $2.7B, up 18% year over year and 15% ex-FX, above the high end of guidance. GBV grew 19% to $29.2B, driven by nights growth, ADR and FX.

Consensus:
Consensus was around $2.62B for revenue and $0.30-$0.31 for EPS based on the scout and transcript summary; revenue beat, EPS missed.

EPS / Earnings:
GAAP EPS was $0.26 and net income was $160M. Net income included both a roughly $70M one-time gain from a private equity investment sale and a roughly $70M one-time deferred-tax-asset adjustment from U.S. Corporate Alternative Minimum Tax changes, so the clean EPS bridge is mixed rather than a simple tax-noise add-back.

Profitability / Margins:
Adjusted EBITDA was $519M, up 24% from $417M, with adjusted EBITDA margin of 19% versus 18% a year earlier. Free cash flow was $1.7B with a 64% margin, although management noted working-capital timing effects from Reserve Now Pay Later.

Key KPI:
The best KPI is the combination of GBV +19%, nights and seats +9%, app-booked nights +22%, and first-time bookers +10%. This points to demand and product engagement rather than only price / FX.

Guidance headline:
Q2 revenue guide of $3.54B-$3.60B implies 14%-16% growth, including around 3% FX tailwind, and management expects Q2 nights growth to decelerate slightly partly due to a roughly 100bp Middle East conflict headwind. Full-year 2026 revenue growth guidance was raised to low-to-mid teens and adjusted EBITDA margin is expected to be at least 35%.

Capital allocation:
Airbnb repurchased $1.1B of Class A stock in Q1 and had $4.5B of remaining authorization as of March 31, 2026. It also held $12.1B of cash, cash equivalents, short-term investments and restricted cash, completed a $2.5B senior unsecured debt offering, and repaid $2.0B of convertible senior notes.

ACCOUNTING / EPS QUALITY CHECK

Reported earnings quality:
Mixed.

Adjustment issue:
Official disclosure shows both a roughly $70M private-equity-investment sale gain and a roughly $70M deferred-tax-asset adjustment tied to U.S. Corporate Alternative Minimum Tax changes; these offset directionally and make the EPS miss less clean than the operating KPIs.

PM interpretation:
Investors should focus on revenue growth, GBV, nights and seats, adjusted EBITDA, FCF timing, app penetration and guidance, while treating GAAP EPS as noisy rather than the dominant signal.

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

The guide is constructive: Q2 revenue of $3.54B-$3.60B, FY26 revenue growth raised to low-to-mid teens, and FY adjusted EBITDA margin at least 35%. The caveat is that Q2 nights growth is expected to decelerate slightly and Middle East conflict is assumed to create an estimated roughly 100bp headwind. Revision direction: upward revisions for revenue and likely adjusted EBITDA, with mixed-to-limited EPS revisions because GAAP tax / one-time items and reinvestment complicate the bottom-line bridge.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

The market had to choose between a clean operating acceleration and noisy EPS optics. The correct read is that the operating model improved: GBV, nights, app mix, first-time bookers, expansion markets, adjusted EBITDA and the guide all point in the same direction.

The reason the stock did not get a cleaner rerating is setup. Travel platforms are being valued on durable demand, take-rate expansion, FCF conversion and international runway. Airbnb improved the evidence on all four, but the EPS miss and one-time-accounting complexity gave investors a reason to avoid chasing.

The forward catalysts are unusually dense: Summer Release, World Cup, continued Reserve Now Pay Later rollout, fee simplification, insurance monetization and AI customer-support productivity. These can sustain a better growth narrative, but they also raise execution expectations.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The strongest signal is broad-based operating acceleration: revenue above guide, GBV +19%, app-booked nights +22%, first-time bookers +10%, and guidance raised. The weakest signal is GAAP EPS readability because the quarter contains both a one-time gain and a one-time tax adjustment.

Analysts are likely to raise revenue and perhaps adjusted EBITDA assumptions, but they may avoid aggressive EPS upgrades until tax-rate, reinvestment and working-capital timing are cleaner. Consensus may still be too conservative on top-line growth if expansion markets and product initiatives continue to compound.

State transition: Improvement

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether Airbnb’s product rebuild could restart growth without sacrificing profitability. Q1 strengthens that case, especially through app penetration, first-time bookers and guide raise. The open question is whether this becomes a sustained new growth phase or a temporary FX / pricing-assisted acceleration.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

Print reaction function:
The market was pricing a premium travel marketplace with improving product velocity and recent analyst support. The reaction makes sense: the operating print was good enough to validate the bull case, but not clean enough to force immediate rerating because EPS optics and valuation created room for profit-taking. The next rerating condition is confirmation that Summer Release, World Cup demand and expansion markets translate into durable nights growth and take-rate upside. The thesis breaks if Middle East cancellations broaden, fee simplification creates host friction, or FCF timing weakens more than expected.

Stock identity:
quality compounder, GARP, expensive but deliverable, setup-risk stock. The print reinforces the quality compounder and GARP identities because growth and EBITDA scaled together, but the premium setup remains fragile when EPS optics are messy and expectations already price successful innovation.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

The reaction was mixed: the stock did not reject the operating print, but it also did not fully reward the beat-and-raise. For peers, the read-across is positive for global travel demand and online booking platforms, but less clean for hotels and OTAs because Airbnb’s supply model, Reserve Now Pay Later, app mix and experiences / services expansion are company-specific.

The broader consumer read is also constructive: travel demand remains resilient, but geopolitical disruption is now measurable in cancellations and has to be built into Q2 expectations.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

Summer Release / Why it matters: It is the next catalyst for whether new categories can expand the platform beyond stays.

World Cup demand / Why it matters: Event-driven travel can validate Airbnb’s ability to capture peak-period accommodation demand.

Middle East cancellation drag / Why it matters: A 100bp headwind is manageable; a larger drag would challenge the resilience narrative.

Reserve Now Pay Later / Why it matters: It supports conversion and lead times but changes working-capital timing.

Tax and one-time items / Why it matters: EPS readability must improve for the market to fully credit operating leverage.

BOTTOM LINE

Airbnb improved operationally.

The guide raise matters more than the EPS miss.

The accounting bridge is noisy and not a one-way tax add-back.

The next proof point is whether product velocity converts into sustained nights growth and take-rate lift.
