<<COIN_EARNINGS_BEGIN>>
TICKER: COIN
COMPANY: Coinbase Global, Inc.
SECTOR: Financials / Information Technology
SUBSECTOR: Crypto Exchange / Onchain Infrastructure / Stablecoins / Derivatives / Prediction Markets
COUNTRY: United States
PUBLICATION_DATE: 2026-05-07
EVENT: Q1 2026
STATE_TRANSITION: Deterioration
IMPORTANCE: ****
MARKET_REACTION: Negative
MARKET_REACTION_DETAIL: Shares fell after-hours and into the next session as revenue, transaction revenue, subscription revenue and GAAP EPS missed while multiple analysts cut price targets.
PM_READ: Coinbase deteriorated near term because both transaction and subscription revenue missed and adjusted EBITDA compressed sharply, but the long-term platform thesis remains alive through record market share, derivatives, prediction markets, Base and regulatory optionality.
THEMES: Crypto cycle, transaction revenue, subscription and services, stablecoins, USDC, Base, derivatives, prediction markets, crypto MTM loss, AI-native finance, workforce restructuring, CLARITY Act, Robinhood competition
SEGMENT_TABLE_STATUS: Partial
SOURCE_ROLE: Full note

EXEC SUMMARY

Deterioration. Coinbase missed on the core earnings lines: total revenue was about $1.4B, net loss was $394M, adjusted EBITDA was $303M, transaction revenue was $756M and subscription and services revenue was $584M. The company still produced its 13th consecutive quarter of positive adjusted EBITDA, but adjusted EBITDA was materially lower than the prior year and the operating revenue miss was not just accounting noise.

The GAAP loss was heavily distorted by $482.4M of losses on crypto assets held for investment, which Coinbase excludes from adjusted EBITDA and adjusted net income because it treats those holdings as long-term investments rather than core operating activity. That explains the scale of the GAAP loss, but it does not erase the weaker trading and subscription revenue.

Strategically, the quarter was not a thesis kill. Coinbase reported all-time-high crypto trading volume market share, 169% YoY derivatives trading volume growth, prediction markets scaling to over $100M annualized revenue in less than two months, Base processing 62% of onchain stablecoin transaction volume, and more than 90% share of onchain agentic stablecoin transaction volume.

OFFICIAL EARNINGS DETAIL

Revenue / Sales:
Coinbase generated around $1.4B of revenue, down 21% sequentially according to the transcript summary, with transaction revenue of $756M and subscription and services revenue of $584M.

Consensus:
Consensus ranges in the scout show revenue expectations around $1.48B-$1.52B and EPS expectations between +$0.04 and +$0.27. The quarter missed on both revenue and GAAP EPS.

EPS / Earnings:
Coinbase reported a net loss of $394M. The non-GAAP bridge shows $482.4M of losses on crypto assets held for investment, a major driver of the GAAP loss and a key reason adjusted EBITDA remained positive at $303.3M.

Profitability / Margins:
Adjusted EBITDA was $303.3M versus $929.9M in Q1 2025 and $565.9M in Q4 2025. Coinbase still delivered positive adjusted EBITDA for the 13th consecutive quarter, but the trajectory is a clear profitability deterioration.

Key KPI:
The best strategic KPIs are 8.6% crypto trading volume market share, 169% derivatives trading volume growth, Base’s 62% share of onchain stablecoin transactions, and prediction markets at over $100M annualized revenue in less than two months.

Guidance headline:
Q2 transaction revenue was running around $215M through early May per the scout, and subscription and services guidance was $565M-$645M. The guide is not a clear upward-revision setup because trading activity remains the swing factor.

Capital allocation:
Coinbase has a substantial liquidity position, but capital return was not the central issue. The more important capital-allocation signal is continued investment in derivatives, prediction markets, Base, stablecoin infrastructure and AI-native finance while restructuring the cost base.

ACCOUNTING / EPS QUALITY CHECK

Reported earnings quality:
Distorted.

Adjustment issue:
The major distortion was $482.4M of losses on crypto assets held for investment; Coinbase excludes gains and losses on these holdings from adjusted EBITDA because it classifies them as non-core operating activity.

PM interpretation:
Investors should not use GAAP EPS alone for the quarter. The cleaner measures are transaction revenue, subscription and services revenue, adjusted EBITDA, trading market share, stablecoin economics and whether new revenue streams reduce dependence on the crypto spot cycle.

SEGMENT / BUSINESS BREAKDOWN

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

The forward setup is mixed. Q2 transaction revenue run-rate through early May did not by itself imply a sharp rebound, while subscription and services guidance of $565M-$645M gives a broad range rather than a clean acceleration signal. Revision direction: downward revisions near term, especially to revenue, adjusted EBITDA and GAAP EPS, partly offset by unchanged or higher strategic optionality assigned to derivatives, prediction markets, Base and stablecoin infrastructure.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

This is a classic crypto-cycle versus infrastructure-platform quarter. Bears can point to missed revenue, missed EPS, lower transaction revenue, lower subscription revenue and a sharp EBITDA reset. Bulls can point to market-share gains, product expansion, derivatives, prediction markets, stablecoins, Base and potential regulatory catalysts.

The stock reaction was rational because the operating miss was real. The market did not overreact to the GAAP loss alone; it also discounted weaker trading activity and the fact that diversification did not yet fully offset crypto-cycle pressure.

The long-term debate remains open. Coinbase is less fragile than a pure spot-crypto broker if Everything Exchange, Base, USDC and prediction markets keep scaling. But the Q1 print shows that the market has not yet been given enough evidence to underwrite the business as a stable financial-infrastructure compounder.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality signal is market-share and product expansion in a weak market. The weakest signal is that both transaction and subscription revenue missed at the same time, while adjusted EBITDA fell sharply.

Analysts are likely to cut near-term revenue and EBITDA assumptions, but keep optionality in out-years where regulatory clarity, stablecoins, derivatives and prediction markets can drive a different mix. Consensus is likely too high for near-term operating leverage but not necessarily too high for long-term platform optionality.

State transition: Deterioration

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether Coinbase was becoming less cycle-dependent. Q1 weakens the near-term answer because core revenue missed and EBITDA reset lower. It does not eliminate the long-term platform case, but it raises the burden of proof.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

Print reaction function:
The market was pricing Coinbase as a high-beta crypto proxy with a growing infrastructure option. The print forced a near-term expectations reset because revenue weakness and EBITDA compression were genuine, while the GAAP loss was only partly explainable by non-operating crypto MTM. For rerating, investors need a BTC / crypto-volume recovery, evidence that derivatives and prediction markets are scaling into material revenue, and regulatory progress. The thesis breaks if Robinhood and other platforms erode share, regulatory catalysts slip, or subscription and services fail to stabilize through the crypto cycle.

Stock identity:
optionality stock, secular growth at a cyclical multiple, setup-risk stock, crowded long. The quarter challenges the secular-growth identity in the near term because operating revenue missed, but it reinforces optionality through Base, stablecoins, prediction markets and derivatives.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

The negative reaction and price-target compression were driven by both the headline miss and a real operating reset. The peer read-across is negative for crypto-exchange earnings sensitivity and positive for platforms with derivatives, stablecoins and tokenization optionality.

For Robinhood and other retail trading platforms, the read-through is mixed: Coinbase still gains crypto market share, but the quarter highlights how quickly trading economics deteriorate when crypto activity softens.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

Crypto trading volume / Why it matters: Transaction revenue remains the main cyclical earnings driver.

Subscription and services stability / Why it matters: This line must prove diversification from spot trading.

Base and stablecoins / Why it matters: Infrastructure economics are the clearest path to lower-cycle revenue.

Prediction markets / Why it matters: Early annualized revenue is promising but needs durability and regulatory clarity.

Adjusted EBITDA / Why it matters: Positive EBITDA streak is intact, but the magnitude of compression matters for valuation.

BOTTOM LINE

Coinbase deteriorated near term.

The GAAP loss is distorted, but the operating miss is real.

Strategic optionality is still high.

The stock now needs 2H26 proof from crypto volumes, regulation, derivatives, prediction markets and Base.

MARKET_REACTION: Negative

PM_READ: MercadoLibre’s top-line engine accelerated materially, but management effectively confirmed that margin recovery is not the near-term objective, making the stock a duration call on credit, logistics and market-share investments.

EXEC SUMMARY

Continuation. MercadoLibre delivered a very strong growth quarter: net revenue and financial income rose 49% to $8.8B, the fastest growth since Q2 2022, while GMV reached $19B and TPV / fintech momentum remained robust. Net income fell to $417M and operating margin compressed to 6.9%, reflecting deliberate investment in logistics, free shipping and credit expansion.

The market reaction was driven by the margin doctrine, not by the revenue line. The company’s profit decline reflected continued investment for market-share gains, while Brazil free-shipping threshold cuts, fintech expansion and credit-card growth were central drivers of the growth / margin trade-off.

This is not a thesis break; it is the formalization of a lower-margin investment phase. Bulls can point to accelerating revenue, Brazil buyer growth, GMV and credit expansion; bears can point to operating margin compression, credit provisioning, and management’s willingness to sacrifice short-term profits for scale.

OFFICIAL EARNINGS DETAIL

Revenue / Sales:
MercadoLibre reported net revenue and financial income of $8.8B, up 49% YoY and described by the company as its fastest growth since Q2 2022. The revenue line was a clear beat.

Consensus:
Consensus ranges in the scout show revenue expectations of $8.36B-$8.47B and adjusted EPS expectations of $8.32-$8.83. Revenue beat, while EPS missed.

EPS / Earnings:
Net income was $417M, down 15.6% from a year earlier and below expectations, with the profit decline attributed to investments in logistics, credit expansion and free shipping.

Profitability / Margins:
Operating income was $611M with a 6.9% operating margin versus 12.9% in Q1 2025. The margin compression is not a one-off accounting item; it is the economic cost of a deliberate investment cycle.

Key KPI:
The key KPI is the interaction between Brazil marketplace growth and credit expansion. GMV was $19B, Brazil unique buyers were up strongly, Brazil sold items rose materially, Mercado Pago MAUs reached 83M, AUM was near $20B, credit portfolio rose 87% to $14.6B, and credit-card book more than doubled to $6.6B.

Guidance headline:
No conventional annual financial guide is the center of the debate. The effective guide is management’s willingness to keep investing and accept near-term profit sacrifice for market-share gains.

Capital allocation:
Capital allocation is being directed toward growth investment rather than near-term margin maximization, including logistics, free shipping, credit expansion and the Argentina investment plan referenced in the scout.

ACCOUNTING / EPS QUALITY CHECK

Reported earnings quality:
Clean but lower quality for near-term EPS.

Adjustment issue:
No single dominant non-operating item explains the miss. The compression is operational, driven by credit provisioning, logistics, free shipping, first-party / cross-border investment and strategic growth spend.

PM interpretation:
Investors should focus on revenue growth, GMV, TPV, credit-book quality, NPLs, provisioning, operating margin and whether the credit / logistics investments convert into higher lifetime value and future margin recovery.

SEGMENT / BUSINESS BREAKDOWN

| Segment | % of Total | Revenue | YoY Growth | Margin Trend | Key Commentary |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| Commerce / Marketplace | Not disclosed | Not disclosed | GMV +42% YoY | Margin pressured by free shipping and investment | Brazil growth and lowered free-shipping thresholds drove volume and share gains. |
| Mercado Pago / Fintech | Not disclosed | $4B net revenue per scout | +51% YoY | Pressured by credit provisioning | Payment volume, AUM and credit-card expansion remain major growth drivers. |
| Credit portfolio | Not disclosed | $14.6B portfolio | +87% YoY | Provisioning-intensive | Credit growth creates upfront margin pressure but potentially high lifetime value. |
| Brazil | Not disclosed | Not disclosed | Unique buyers +32%; sold items +56% | Investment-heavy | Brazil is the core battleground and the clearest proof point for the growth strategy. |
| Argentina | Not disclosed | Not disclosed | +65% FX-neutral consolidated revenue per scout | Investment phase | Growth is normalizing but remains high; investment plan keeps strategic importance elevated. |

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

MercadoLibre’s effective guidance is a strategic posture: management is willing to sacrifice short-term profit to pursue market share across logistics, credit, free shipping and fintech. Revision direction: mix-shift revisions, with revenue estimates likely revised up or sustained, but operating margin and EPS estimates likely revised down or pushed out because the company is not prioritizing near-term margin recovery.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

The selloff was fundamental, not just positioning. MercadoLibre did not miss because demand was weak; it missed because the company is choosing to buy growth through credit, free shipping, logistics and category expansion.

That distinction matters. A weak-demand selloff would challenge the platform model. This selloff challenges the valuation duration and the timing of margin recovery. The stock can still work, but the required holding period extends and the next proof points shift from revenue acceleration to credit seasoning and margin stabilization.

The peer read-across is relevant for any marketplace competing against Amazon, Shein or local fintech challengers: scale investment can still drive growth, but public markets are less willing to capitalize indefinite margin compression without evidence of risk discipline.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality signal is the revenue acceleration and Brazil marketplace / fintech growth. The lowest-quality signal is that the margin decline is structural and deliberate, not temporary noise.

Analysts are likely to raise or preserve revenue trajectories but cut near-term EPS and operating-margin assumptions. The debate becomes whether the credit book, free shipping and logistics investments generate enough future cash flow to justify lower margins through 2026 and possibly into 2027.

State transition: Continuation

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was already about growth investment versus margin recovery. Q1 confirms both sides: the growth engine is stronger than expected, but margin recovery is further away. The thesis continues, but with a more explicit duration burden.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

Print reaction function:
The market was pricing a premium LATAM compounder with some hope that revenue growth could coexist with gradual margin repair. The print broke that near-term margin repair hope but strengthened the growth story. The reaction makes sense because the valuation bridge depends on when margins normalize. For rerating, MELI needs stable credit quality, evidence that Brazil free-shipping changes generate repeat purchase behavior, and signs that provision growth can season into interest income. The thesis breaks if credit losses rise faster than expected or margin compression fails to produce durable share gains.

Stock identity:
high-growth compounder, GARP, expensive but deliverable, setup-risk stock. The print reinforces the high-growth compounder identity but challenges the expensive-but-deliverable identity because delivery now requires patience on margins and credit-cycle discipline.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

The sharp negative reaction reflects a repricing of the P&L bridge rather than a rejection of marketplace demand. For peer read-across, the quarter is positive for LATAM e-commerce and fintech demand, but negative for margin expectations in competitive marketplace models.

The read-through for global marketplaces is that aggressive reinvestment still works on revenue, but investors are increasingly sensitive to how much operating leverage is deferred.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

Brazil credit book seasoning / Why it matters: Credit growth is the largest upside / downside swing factor for margins and risk perception.

NPLs and coverage / Why it matters: Stable asset quality is essential if the market is to tolerate rapid credit expansion.

Free-shipping ROI / Why it matters: Lower thresholds must produce durable buyer frequency and category share gains.

Operating margin floor / Why it matters: Investors need evidence that 6.9% is near trough rather than a moving lower baseline.

GenAI search deployment / Why it matters: Better conversion and ad returns could partially offset investment-driven margin pressure.

BOTTOM LINE

MercadoLibre’s growth engine accelerated.

The margin bridge moved further out.

This is not a broken platform story.

It is a longer-duration, credit-sensitive, investment-cycle continuation.

TICKER: VST

SUBSECTOR: Independent Power Producer / Merchant Generation / Nuclear / Gas / AI Power Infrastructure
COUNTRY: United States
PUBLICATION_DATE: 2026-05-07
EVENT: Q1 2026
STATE_TRANSITION: Improvement
IMPORTANCE: ****
MARKET_REACTION: Negative
MARKET_REACTION_DETAIL: Shares fell about 4% despite record Q1 EBITDA and reaffirmed guidance because the major upside catalysts remain outside current guidance.
PM_READ: Vistra’s record Q1 operational execution strengthens the AI-power thesis, but the stock still needs Cogentrix closing and Meta PPA integration into guidance before the embedded option value is fully monetized.
THEMES: AI power demand, nuclear PPAs, Meta PPA, Cogentrix acquisition, merchant power, hedging, fleet reliability, buybacks, coal retirement, power scarcity, data-center electricity
SEGMENT_TABLE_STATUS: Partial
SOURCE_ROLE: Full note

EXEC SUMMARY

Improvement. Vistra delivered a record Q1, with operating revenues of $5.64B, GAAP EPS of $2.87, net income of $1.029B, ongoing operations adjusted EBITDA of $1.494B and operating cash flow of $1.199B. The quarter validated the operational side of the thesis: strong generation performance, winter-storm reliability, and commercial optimization offset mild-weather pressure in retail.

The market did not reward the print because the decisive catalysts are still ahead. FY26 adjusted EBITDA guidance of $6.8B-$7.6B and adjusted free cash flow before growth guidance of $3.925B-$4.725B were reaffirmed, but Cogentrix and the Meta nuclear PPAs are not yet embedded in the guidance framework. The result is an in-line operational beat with embedded future upside rather than an immediate revision event.

The key PM issue is not whether AI power demand is real; the Meta PPA and pending Cogentrix acquisition support that. The issue is sequencing: investors need closure, integration, financing confidence and 2027 guidance incorporation before the stock can rerate cleanly again.

OFFICIAL EARNINGS DETAIL

Revenue / Sales:
Operating revenues were $5.640B, up materially from $3.933B in Q1 2025. Revenue was above the scout’s consensus range and reflected stronger generation performance, commercial optimization and favorable portfolio conditions.

Consensus:
Consensus was mixed across sources, with revenue expectations around $5.22B-$5.40B and EPS expectations around $1.94-$2.21. Vistra beat the headline revenue and EPS bars.

EPS / Earnings:
GAAP diluted EPS was $2.87 and net income was $1.029B, a major swing from a Q1 2025 net loss of $268M. The EPS beat should be treated carefully because GAAP results included unrealized hedge gains expected to settle in future years.

Profitability / Margins:
Ongoing operations adjusted EBITDA was $1.494B, described in the scout as record Q1 EBITDA. This is the cleaner operating measure for PM work because it strips out some GAAP volatility and better captures fleet and commercial execution.

Key KPI:
The dominant KPI is ongoing operations adjusted EBITDA, followed by operating cash flow and fleet availability. Natural gas availability of 97% and nuclear availability of 100% during Winter Storm Fern are central to the reliability and AI-power supplier thesis.

Guidance headline:
FY26 adjusted EBITDA guidance was reaffirmed at $6.8B-$7.6B and adjusted free cash flow before growth at $3.925B-$4.725B. The guide excludes the pending Cogentrix acquisition and Meta PPA contribution, which makes current guidance conservative relative to the strategic pipeline.

Capital allocation:
Vistra repurchased $372M of shares in Q1 and about $525M through the first four months of 2026, with about $1.5B of authorization remaining. It also issued substantial debt to extend maturities and fund the next phase of growth and M&A.

ACCOUNTING / EPS QUALITY CHECK

Reported earnings quality:
Mixed.

Adjustment issue:
GAAP EPS includes unrealized hedge gains, which inflate the headline EPS beat relative to clean operating power earnings.

PM interpretation:
Investors should focus on ongoing operations adjusted EBITDA, operating cash flow, hedging, FCF before growth, capital allocation, Cogentrix integration and contracted PPA economics rather than GAAP EPS alone.

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

FY26 guidance was reaffirmed rather than raised, which explains why the stock did not rerate despite the record Q1. The embedded upside sits outside current guidance: Cogentrix is targeted for H2 2026 close and the Meta PPA contribution is not yet fully in the model. Revision direction: no material consensus change near term, with potential upward revisions later in 2026 if Cogentrix closes on schedule and management incorporates Meta PPA economics into the 2027 framework.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

The market treated Vistra as an execution-confirming but catalyst-deferring print. The operational numbers were strong, but the stock needed a guidance bridge on Cogentrix or Meta PPA economics to force a positive revision cycle. Without that, investors could sell the print even though the fundamental thesis improved.

The AI-power narrative remains intact. Vistra is no longer just a merchant IPP with strong buybacks; it is becoming a scarce, contracted power supplier for hyperscaler demand. The market is now asking whether the balance sheet, integration capacity and regulatory path can support the next leg.

The negative reaction is therefore not a rejection of the AI-power thesis. It is a timing and valuation reset around when embedded option value becomes earnings guidance.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality signal is record Q1 adjusted EBITDA combined with high gas and nuclear reliability. The lowest-quality signal is GAAP EPS, because unrealized hedge gains distort the magnitude of the beat.

Analysts are likely to leave FY26 broadly intact but focus on the 2027 bridge, Cogentrix closing assumptions, Meta PPA contribution, balance-sheet capacity and coal retirement economics. Consensus may be too low beyond 2026 if the contracted-power backlog converts, but not necessarily too low for the current-year guide.

State transition: Improvement

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether Vistra could sustain its AI-power rerating while integrating large strategic growth opportunities. Q1 improves confidence in operating execution but does not yet solve the timing of guidance recognition. The thesis improves, but the market still needs a conversion event.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

Print reaction function:
The market was pricing an AI-power winner with embedded PPA and M&A upside. The record EBITDA print confirmed the base business, but the move lower makes sense because the upside catalysts are not yet in numbers. For rerating, Vistra needs Cogentrix close, 2027 guidance integration, stable leverage optics and continued fleet reliability. The thesis breaks if integration risk rises, power-price assumptions weaken, or debt funding starts to crowd out capital return.

Stock identity:
secular growth at a cyclical multiple, quality compounder, crowded long, setup-risk stock. The print reinforces the scarce AI-power compounder identity, but the negative reaction shows that the stock is now priced around catalyst conversion rather than simple earnings beats.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

The stock fell around 4% despite a record Q1 because the current guide did not include the major strategic upside. The peer read-across is constructive for IPPs and nuclear-linked power suppliers, but it also warns that AI-power stocks need guidance conversion and balance-sheet clarity to sustain valuation premiums.

For CEG, NRG, TLN and other power-exposed names, the lesson is that AI PPA announcements are no longer enough by themselves; the market wants earnings translation.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

Cogentrix close / Why it matters: The acquisition is the next major growth and guidance-integration catalyst.

Meta PPA economics / Why it matters: Investors need visibility on contracted nuclear revenue, margins and timing.

Leverage and refinancing / Why it matters: Debt issuance supports growth but can cap valuation if funding risk rises.

Fleet reliability / Why it matters: AI-power customers pay for dependable capacity, not just nameplate MW.

Coal retirement and repowering / Why it matters: Retiring 4,578 MW by 2028 changes mix and creates conversion optionality.

BOTTOM LINE

Vistra’s operating print improved the thesis.

The stock reaction was about timing, not broken fundamentals.

GAAP EPS is not the clean metric because hedge gains distort the beat.

The next leg depends on Cogentrix, Meta PPA integration and 2027 guidance conversion.

Reversal. Datadog delivered a clean beat-and-raise: revenue crossed $1B for the first time, grew 32% YoY, accelerated from the prior quarter, and exceeded guidance. Non-GAAP EPS of $0.60 beat consensus materially, operating cash flow was $335M, free cash flow was $289M and FY26 revenue guidance was raised to $4.30B-$4.34B.

The key signal is not just AI customer demand. AI is now additive to a still-healthy digital-transformation base.

The stock’s about 31% surge and sympathy moves in Snowflake and MongoDB show that the market treated this as a bucket-level re-rating event for AI-adjacent enterprise software. This was not a setup-driven bounce; it was a fundamental reversal of the demand-quality debate.

The scout indicates consensus revenue around $960M and non-GAAP EPS expectations around $0.42-$0.50. Datadog beat both meaningfully.

EPS / Earnings:
Non-GAAP EPS was $0.60. The beat appears operational, driven by revenue acceleration and operating leverage rather than a disclosed one-time accounting item.

Profitability / Margins:
Operating cash flow was $335M and free cash flow was $289M. The free-cash-flow profile is important because Datadog is being valued not only on revenue acceleration but also on whether AI software demand can generate durable operating leverage.

Key KPI:
The dominant KPI is non-AI customer growth reaccelerating to the mid-20% range. This matters because it proves Datadog is not only riding AI-native customer demand; traditional enterprise cloud migration and product adoption are also improving.

Guidance headline:
Q2 revenue guidance was $1.07B-$1.08B, implying 29%-31% growth. FY26 revenue guidance was raised to $4.30B-$4.34B from $4.06B-$4.10B, and FY26 non-GAAP EPS guidance was raised to $2.36-$2.44.

Capital allocation:
Capital allocation was not the main event. The key capital-allocation equivalent is product investment across AI observability, security, GPU monitoring, MCP Server and agentic security workflows.

Clean.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

Datadog is the cleanest positive software print in the Thursday cohort. The market was looking for proof that AI infrastructure spending creates monetizable software demand, and Datadog delivered it through revenue reacceleration, non-AI customer strength and a large full-year guide raise.

The sympathy moves in Snowflake and MongoDB matter. They show that investors were not just rewarding Datadog company-specific execution; they were rebuilding exposure to AI-adjacent enterprise software after a period where the AI trade was concentrated in semis and hyperscalers.

The quarter changes the debate from whether usage-based software is still healthy to how much multiple premium Datadog deserves as AI workloads move into production.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality signal is the combination of non-AI customer growth in the mid-20% range and AI integration adoption across customers representing 80% of ARR. That mix refutes the most important bear case: that Datadog was becoming too dependent on AI-native demand.

The lowest-quality signal is valuation sensitivity after a 31% move, not the quarter itself. Analysts are likely to raise models, but the next debate will shift to whether the new guide is conservative enough and whether FCF margins can keep expanding while product investment remains high.

State transition: Reversal

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether usage-based cloud software demand was normalizing after AI-led optimism. Q1 reverses that concern by showing accelerating revenue, broad enterprise demand and formal AI-pillar positioning. The thesis shifts from defense to renewed growth leadership.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

Print reaction function:
The market was pricing uncertainty around whether observability demand was still broad or only AI-native. The print resolved that asymmetry positively: revenue accelerated, non-AI customer growth improved, guidance rose and adjacent software peers rallied in sympathy. For rerating to continue, Datadog needs sustained non-AI growth, continued AI workload monetization and FCF resilience. The thesis breaks if usage-based growth decelerates again or AI product adoption fails to convert into durable ARR expansion.

Stock identity:
high-growth compounder, beat-and-raise machine, secular growth at a cyclical multiple, expensive but deliverable. The print strongly reinforces the beat-and-raise and high-growth compounder identity, while the magnitude of the move means the stock is again vulnerable to valuation if the next print is merely good rather than exceptional.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

The about 31% rally was driven by a clean beat-and-raise and a major bear-thesis reversal. Snowflake and MongoDB sympathy moves indicate a broader institutional rotation into AI-adjacent infrastructure software.

Peer read-across is positive for observability, data infrastructure, cloud databases, security and developer platforms. It is especially relevant for names that can prove AI usage is additive to traditional enterprise demand rather than cannibalizing it.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

Non-AI customer growth / Why it matters: This is the clearest test of whether Datadog is a broad cloud compounder rather than only an AI workload derivative.

AI integration penetration / Why it matters: Customers representing 80% of ARR using AI integrations creates a large monetization runway.

New product ARR / Why it matters: The 18 early lifecycle products need to scale into the next cross-sell engine.

FCF margin / Why it matters: High-growth software must show operating leverage to sustain a premium multiple.

Sympathy basket persistence / Why it matters: SNOW and MDB reactions show bucket-level positioning that can amplify both upside and downside.

BOTTOM LINE

Datadog delivered a clean reversal print.

The AI software monetization thesis is now better supported by data.

Non-AI customer acceleration is the most important signal.

The stock move was large, but fundamentally justified by the scale of the beat and guide raise.

| Adjusted EBITDA margin | 56% | 62% prior year | Down 6pp | Margin compression matters despite strong revenue growth. |
| Adjusted operating income | $21M | $163M prior year | Down sharply | Depreciation and cost structure are pressuring operating economics. |
| Interest expense | $536M | Prior year about half this level | Doubled YoY | Financing cost is now the central bear variable. |
| Total debt | About $25B | Prior not confirmed | Elevated | Leverage is the price of scaling AI capacity quickly. |
| Contracted power | About 3.5 GW total; above 1 GW active | Prior not confirmed | Expanded | Power access is a strategic bottleneck and differentiator. |

OFFICIAL EARNINGS DETAIL

Revenue / Sales:
Revenue was $2.08B, up 111.6% YoY and 32% sequentially, above the $1.97B consensus cited in the scout. The revenue beat confirms demand for CoreWeave capacity remains extremely strong.

Consensus:
Consensus was around $1.97B for revenue and around -$0.90 for adjusted EPS. Revenue beat, but adjusted EPS missed and Q2 revenue guidance was below the consensus cited in the scout.

EPS / Earnings:
Adjusted EPS was -$1.12 versus around -$0.90 expected, while GAAP net loss was -$740M. Diluted net loss per share was -$1.40, slightly better than -$1.49 in Q1 2025 according to the scout, but the absolute GAAP loss remains large.

Profitability / Margins:
Adjusted EBITDA was $1.16B with a 56% margin, but adjusted operating income was only $21M, or about 1% margin, down from $163M and 17% margin in Q1 2025. This gap captures the core tension between EBITDA-scale demand and depreciation / financing-heavy economics.

Key KPI:
Revenue backlog of $99.4B is the dominant KPI. It is the clearest evidence that CoreWeave is not demand-constrained; it is execution, power, capex and financing constrained.

Guidance headline:
Q2 revenue guidance of $2.45B-$2.60B was below the $2.69B consensus cited in the scout. FY26 revenue guidance was maintained at $12B-$13B, FY26 capex was raised to $31B-$35B, 2026 exit ARR was raised to $18B-$19B, and 2027 ARR target above $30B was reiterated.

Capital allocation:
Capital allocation is dominated by capacity buildout. The company raised more than $20B of debt and equity year to date, had about $25B of debt at quarter-end, and is relying on large-scale financing structures to convert contracted AI infrastructure demand into deployed capacity.

ACCOUNTING / EPS QUALITY CHECK

Reported earnings quality:
Distorted.

Adjustment issue:
GAAP net loss is structurally affected by high depreciation, interest expense and financing costs linked to the AI capacity buildout; Q1 interest expense of $536M alone explains a large share of the GAAP loss.

PM interpretation:
Investors should focus on revenue backlog, capacity deployment, adjusted EBITDA, adjusted operating income, interest expense, capex, power availability and contract conversion rather than GAAP EPS alone.

SEGMENT / BUSINESS BREAKDOWN

SEGMENT TABLE

| Segment | % of Total | Revenue | YoY Growth | Margin Trend | Key Commentary |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| GPU cloud infrastructure | Not disclosed | Not disclosed | Very strong | EBITDA-positive | Core business remains demand-constrained with 2026 capacity effectively sold out. |
| Hyperscaler and AI labs | Not disclosed | Not disclosed | Strong | Not disclosed | Meta and Anthropic commitments support counterparty diversification. |
| Enterprise verticals | Not disclosed | Not disclosed | Growing | Not disclosed | Financial services backlog approaching $10B per scout indicates broader customer mix. |
| Power and data-center capacity | Not applicable | Not applicable | Contracted power expanding | Capex-intensive | Active power above 1 GW and path toward 8 GW by 2030 are strategic constraints and assets. |
| Financing platform | Not applicable | Not applicable | Scaling | Interest burden rising | Investment-grade customer-backed financing is central to the model. |

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

The guide was the source of the selloff. FY26 revenue was maintained at $12B-$13B and 2026 exit ARR target increased to $18B-$19B, but Q2 revenue guidance was light versus consensus and FY26 capex rose to $31B-$35B. Revision direction: mix-shift revisions, with long-term revenue and backlog confidence intact, but near-term EPS, operating income, FCF and leverage assumptions likely pressured by higher capex and interest expense.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

CoreWeave’s quarter is not a demand problem. The company has nearly $100B of backlog, expanded customer commitments, and capacity that is effectively allocated. The selloff happened because the market is forcing the bull case through a financing lens.

That is the right framework. AI infrastructure can be structurally scarce and still produce equity volatility if the revenue-recognition timeline, interest cost and capex burden move against short-term expectations. CoreWeave is effectively asking investors to underwrite a massive forward backlog through a levered buildout model.

The stock’s pre-print setup also mattered. With shares up about 80% YTD entering the print, a light Q2 guide and capex increase were enough to trigger a sharp reset even though the underlying backlog story improved.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality signal is backlog growth and customer diversification. The weakest signal is the operating-income and interest-expense bridge, because it shows that revenue growth is not yet translating cleanly into equity earnings.

Analysts are likely to preserve long-term revenue optimism but lower near-term margin, EPS and FCF confidence. The key debate is whether CoreWeave can scale capacity at a cost of capital low enough to preserve equity value after debt, depreciation and customer concentration risks.

State transition: Continuation

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether CoreWeave was a scarce AI capacity platform or an overextended financing vehicle. Q1 confirms the scarce-capacity thesis through backlog and customer commitments. It also confirms the financing concern through capex, debt and interest expense.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

Print reaction function:
The market was pricing CoreWeave as a pure AI-infrastructure growth vehicle after a major YTD run. The revenue beat and backlog expansion supported the thesis, but the light Q2 guide, capex increase and interest expense reset the near-term equity math. For rerating, CoreWeave needs Q2 revenue delivery, improving adjusted operating income, stable cost of debt and proof that 2027 ARR remains contracted and financeable. The thesis breaks if interest expense keeps outpacing revenue conversion or if customer concentration and capacity delays weaken backlog quality.

Stock identity:
high-growth compounder, optionality stock, expensive and fragile, setup-risk stock, crowded long. The print reinforces the high-growth and optionality identity, but the stock’s fragility is real because valuation depends on financing the backlog without diluting equity economics.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

The stock fell roughly 10%-15% after-hours and around 12% into the next session. The reaction was driven by the combination of setup, light Q2 guide, capex raise and financing math, not by demand weakness.

Peer read-across is important for AI infrastructure suppliers, data-center power names, GPU financing vehicles and NVIDIA-linked ecosystems. The quarter says AI demand remains strong, but markets will now scrutinize capital intensity and financing cost much more aggressively.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

Q2 revenue delivery / Why it matters: The light guide becomes manageable only if execution lands cleanly.

Interest expense / Why it matters: Financing cost is the main swing factor between backlog value and equity value.

Capex inflation / Why it matters: Higher component and buildout costs can absorb revenue upside.

Customer diversification / Why it matters: More $1B customers reduce dependency on a narrow hyperscaler base.

Power access / Why it matters: Contracted power is a strategic bottleneck and a barrier to entry.

BOTTOM LINE

CoreWeave’s demand signal remains very strong.

The equity problem is financing, not backlog.

The selloff was rational given setup, capex and Q2 guide.

The stock now needs proof that backlog can convert into operating income after debt and depreciation.

<<CRWV_EARNINGS_END>>

<<NET_EARNINGS_BEGIN>>
TICKER: NET
COMPANY: Cloudflare, Inc.
SECTOR: Information Technology
SUBSECTOR: Edge Cloud / Cybersecurity / Developer Platform / AI Infrastructure
COUNTRY: United States
PUBLICATION_DATE: 2026-05-07
EVENT: Q1 2026
STATE_TRANSITION: False dawn
IMPORTANCE: ***
MARKET_REACTION: Negative
MARKET_REACTION_DETAIL: Shares fell more than 20% after a revenue and EPS beat was overwhelmed by a 20% workforce reduction, margin pressure and a slightly soft Q2 revenue guide.
PM_READ: Cloudflare’s Q1 operating beat looked constructive, but the 20% AI-driven restructuring turned the print into a show-me event on execution, margin recovery and whether agentic productivity is truly accretive.
THEMES: AI restructuring, agentic AI, edge cloud, Workers, cybersecurity, gross margin compression, enterprise customers, large deals, operating model evolution, workforce reduction, developer platform, AI traffic
SEGMENT_TABLE_STATUS: Partial
SOURCE_ROLE: Full note

EXEC SUMMARY

False dawn. Cloudflare reported a strong Q1 on the surface: revenue grew 34% to $639.8M, non-GAAP EPS was $0.25, non-GAAP operating income was $73.1M, free cash flow was $84.1M and $100k-plus customers rose 25% YoY. FY26 revenue and EPS guidance were raised.

The market rejected the print because the operating beat was overshadowed by a 20% workforce reduction, $140M-$150M of restructuring charges, a slight Q2 revenue guide shortfall versus consensus and non-GAAP gross margin pressure. The stock’s initial positive reaction reversed into a sharp selloff as investors shifted from Q1 execution to restructuring risk.

This is a major cohort signal. Cloudflare joins the AI-driven workforce-restructuring pattern, but the market response shows that investors are no longer automatically rewarding cuts framed as productivity gains. They want proof that agentic AI reduces cost without damaging growth, execution quality or enterprise momentum.

OFFICIAL EARNINGS DETAIL

Revenue / Sales:
Revenue was $639.8M, up 34% YoY, beating the $620.9M-$628M consensus range cited in the scout. Current RPO grew 34% YoY, supporting the forward revenue base.

Consensus:
The scout indicates revenue consensus around $620.9M-$628M and non-GAAP EPS expectations between $0.17 and $0.24. Cloudflare beat Q1 revenue and EPS, but Q2 revenue guidance was slightly below the consensus cited in the scout.

EPS / Earnings:
Non-GAAP diluted EPS was $0.25 and non-GAAP net income was $94M. GAAP net loss was -$22.9M. The GAAP loss improved versus the -$38.5M prior-year loss based on the figures in the scout, even though investor focus shifted to future restructuring charges.

Profitability / Margins:
Non-GAAP operating income was $73.1M, or 11% of revenue, and free cash flow was $84.1M, or 13% of revenue. Non-GAAP gross margin of 72.8% missed the 75.1% estimate cited in the scout and was pressured by mix and network-cost allocation.

Key KPI:
The central KPI is the interaction between large-customer growth and gross margin. Cloudflare added enterprise scale, but lower-margin developer products and cost allocation pressure created a quality issue.

Guidance headline:
Q2 revenue guidance of $664M-$665M was slightly below consensus, while FY26 revenue guidance of $2.805B-$2.813B and FY26 non-GAAP EPS guidance of $1.19-$1.20 were constructive. The market focused on Q2 and restructuring rather than the FY EPS raise.

Capital allocation:
The defining capital-allocation and operating-model event was the 20% workforce reduction, expected to generate $140M-$150M of restructuring charges, with most charges in Q2 and completion substantially by the end of Q3.

ACCOUNTING / EPS QUALITY CHECK

Reported earnings quality:
Mixed.

Adjustment issue:
Q1 GAAP and non-GAAP differences are manageable, but the announced $140M-$150M restructuring charge will materially distort Q2 and Q3 GAAP results.

PM interpretation:
Investors should focus on revenue growth, large-customer expansion, RPO, gross margin, FCF, Q2 revenue guide, and whether the restructuring translates into durable operating leverage without disrupting execution.

SEGMENT / BUSINESS BREAKDOWN

SEGMENT TABLE

|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---|

GUIDANCE / CAPITAL ALLOCATION

FY26 guidance was raised on revenue and EPS, but Q2 revenue guidance was modestly below consensus and the restructuring introduces near-term execution risk. Revision direction: mix-shift revisions, with FY EPS potentially revised up but near-term revenue confidence, gross margin and GAAP earnings estimates pressured by restructuring charges and execution risk.

PROFESSIONAL MARKET COMMENTARY

Cloudflare’s Q1 was a beat that became a credibility test. The market did not sell the stock because the Q1 numbers were weak. It sold the stock because a 20% workforce cut announced alongside a slightly soft Q2 guide creates uncertainty around execution, culture, sales capacity and whether AI productivity claims can be translated into numbers.

The gross margin miss matters because it gives investors a second reason to demand proof. If the business is shifting toward lower-margin developer and AI products, then restructuring has to do more work to preserve operating leverage.

This is the sharpest example in the cohort of an AI restructuring framed from strength but priced as risk. It changes the market rulebook: investors will not simply capitalize AI-driven headcount cuts unless guidance and margin proof arrive at the same time.

COMMENT — ANALYST LAYER

The highest-quality signal is enterprise momentum: $100k-plus customer growth, current RPO growth, large deals and FY guide improvement. The lowest-quality signal is the combination of gross margin compression, Q2 revenue softness and a very large workforce cut.

Analysts are likely to raise or preserve FY EPS but apply more scrutiny to revenue durability, gross margin, sales productivity and restructuring execution. Consensus may be right on the full-year revenue path, but confidence in the quality of that path has weakened.

State transition: False dawn

CONTEXT FROM PRIOR QUARTER / PRIOR DEBATE

The prior debate was whether Cloudflare could monetize AI traffic and enterprise demand while improving operating leverage. Q1 initially appeared to support that thesis. The restructuring converted the print into a show-me event rather than a clean improvement.

HF TAKE — STRATEGIST LAYER

Print reaction function:
The market was pricing a high-growth edge cloud and AI infrastructure platform. The Q1 beat supported the growth story, but the 20% workforce cut and soft Q2 revenue guide changed the reaction function from growth validation to execution risk. For rerating, Cloudflare needs to prove that restructuring improves sales productivity and margins without slowing enterprise momentum. The thesis breaks if net retention declines further, gross margin pressure persists, or the workforce cut disrupts customer-facing execution.

Stock identity:
high-growth compounder, expensive and fragile, setup-risk stock, optionality stock. The print challenges the high-growth compounder identity because management introduced a large operating-model discontinuity; it reinforces optionality only if agentic AI productivity becomes measurable in margins and FCF.

MARKET REACTION / PEER READ-ACROSS

The stock’s more than 20% selloff was driven by restructuring uncertainty, gross margin pressure and Q2 guide softness, not by the Q1 beat itself. The reaction was rational because the market needs a higher proof threshold when a premium software multiple is paired with a 20% workforce cut.

Peer read-across is important for cybersecurity, edge cloud, software and AI application names. The message is that AI-driven restructuring is not automatically bullish; it must be accompanied by clean guidance, margin expansion and evidence of customer continuity.

KEY ISSUES TO MONITOR

Restructuring execution / Why it matters: A 20% workforce reduction can improve margins or disrupt sales and product execution.

Gross margin / Why it matters: Lower-margin product mix could offset AI productivity gains.

Q2 revenue delivery / Why it matters: The soft guide is now the first test of whether restructuring creates disruption.

Net retention / Why it matters: DBNR at 118% is solid but the QoQ decline needs monitoring.

AI and Workers monetization / Why it matters: The company must prove AI traffic and developer adoption can become durable high-quality revenue.

BOTTOM LINE

Cloudflare beat Q1 numbers but lost the market narrative.

The 20% workforce cut turned strength into uncertainty.

FY guidance is not enough without proof of execution and margin leverage.

This is a false dawn until AI productivity appears in clean operating results.
